folgefonna-glacier-walk-report.title: Folgefonna glacier walk 2026: the Blue Ice Hike above Jondal, crampons at 1,200 meters
folgefonna-glacier-walk-report.subtitle: Norway's third largest glacier sits on a plateau above Hardangerfjord. Folgefonni Glacier Team leads the walks from the Fonna Glacier Ski Resort guide shop. This is the current route, the real prices, and what 400-year-old ice looks like under your feet.
folgefonna-glacier-walk-report.excerpt: Folgefonna covers 207 km² and is Norway's third largest glacier. Guided walks with Folgefonni Glacier Team depart from the guide shop at Fonna Glacier Ski Resort, 19 km from Jondal. Blue Ice Hike from 1,249 NOK, 5 to 6 hours, May to November. This is the logistics and what glacier walking involves.
folgefonna-glacier-walk-report.content:
Hardangerfjord at sea level in July is warm enough to swim in. Around 18°C on a good day. Forty minutes by road above Jondal, at 1,200 meters on the Folgefonna plateau, you are walking on 400-year-old glacial ice with 12-point crampons strapped to your boots. The temperature on the ice is 5 to 8°C even in peak summer. The contrast inside a single day trip is one of the more specific experiences available in any Norwegian fjord region.

Folgefonna covers roughly 207 km² across three connected glaciers (Nordre, Midtre, and Søndre Folgefonna). It is the third largest glacier system in mainland Norway after Jostedalsbreen and Svartisen. The plateau sits in Folgefonna National Park directly above the south shore of Hardangerfjord between Jondal and Odda. Guided access is the only legal way onto the ice. This is what the standard Blue Ice Hike involves.

Getting to the glacier

The access road climbs from Jondal on the north shore of Hardangerfjord to Fonna Glacier Ski Resort at 1,199 meters. Distance from Jondal to the ski centre is 19 km. Drive time is 30 to 40 minutes on a narrow mountain road with passing places.

The toll station sits 8 km above Jondal. The fee for a standard car in 2026 is 120 NOK. Payment is by card at the booth. The road is closed in winter and typically opens late April or early May depending on snow.

From the south shore of Hardangerfjord (Rv550), take the Jondal to Tørvikbygd car ferry. Crossing time is 20 minutes. The ferry runs roughly every hour in summer. Check norled.no for the current timetable and any reduced-service days.

Booking and operator

Folgefonni Glacier Team (folgefonni.no) is the licensed glacier guide operation on Folgefonna. The Juklafjord booking office sits in Jondal. The guide shop for walk departures is at Fonna Glacier Ski Resort at the top of the toll road. Book online in advance for peak season dates. Walks fill in July and August.

The tour options

Three guided walks cover most visitor needs. Prices are 2026 starting rates.

  • Panorama Glacier Hike. From 1,190 NOK. 3 to 4 hours. Easy grade. Runs daily from mid-June to mid-August. This is the entry-level option. The route stays on the flatter part of the plateau and focuses on the views across to Hardangerfjord. Accessible by public transport from Bergen as a day trip with careful connection planning.
  • Blue Ice Hike. From 1,249 NOK. 5 to 6 hours with around 5 to 6 km on the ice and 300 to 400 meters of elevation change on the glacier. Medium grade. Runs daily end of May to November. Age limit 10+. This is the standard full glacier experience with a descent into the Juklavass arm where the blue ice is exposed.
  • Glacier Crossing, 2 days. From 1,590 NOK. Two-day traverse with a DNT cabin overnight. Hard grade. For walkers with hill fitness and prior mountain experience.

Winter options (from 3,490 NOK) and 4-day basic glacier courses (from 7,000 NOK) are also on the folgefonni.no calendar. Private guides run from 9,390 NOK per day for groups of up to roughly 4 to 6 people.

What the Blue Ice Hike actually involves

The guide fits 12-point steel crampons to your boots at the shop. Ice axes and harnesses are issued for the sections where they are required. The group walks to the ice edge on a short approach path. Crampon training starts on flat ice before the route angles down.

Crampons change your gait. You flat-foot down slopes instead of edging, and you walk with your feet slightly wider than normal to avoid catching the points on your opposite gaiter. Ten minutes of practice makes the technique feel natural. The guides demonstrate before the group moves into any steeper terrain.

The Juklavass glacier arm shows the deep blue ice that gives the tour its name. The colour is the result of years of compression squeezing air out of the ice and letting it absorb all wavelengths except blue. The compressed layers you see in a vertical wall represent annual snowfall. A typical wall on the Blue Ice Hike route shows 200 to 400 years of accumulation. Ask the guide to point out the seasonal banding.

Crevasse sections require the harness and a short-rope technique. The guide leads, and the group moves in sequence. Solo walking on the glacier is not permitted and not safe. The crevasse network on Folgefonna is partially snow-bridged and not all hazards are visible from the surface.

What to bring

  • Layered clothing. A base layer in merino or synthetic, a mid-layer fleece, and a waterproof shell. The temperature on the ice runs 10 to 15°C cooler than at the fjord.
  • Stiff boots. The crampons fit best on a stiff mountaineering or 4-season hiking boot. Soft trail runners will not hold crampons properly. The operator can advise on rentals if needed.
  • Sun protection. The glacier reflects UV at 1,200 meters. Sunglasses (ideally Category 3 or higher) and high-SPF sunscreen are essential. Cloud cover does not remove the UV risk.
  • Gloves and hat. Thin liner gloves are useful even in July. A warm hat matters if the wind picks up on the plateau.
  • Packed lunch and water. The guide shop has a small kiosk. No full catering. Minimum 1.5 liters of water.

Fonna Glacier Ski Resort

The same toll road ends at Fonna Glacier Ski Resort, which operates on glacier snow from roughly mid-May through late August. This makes Fonna one of the longest-running summer ski operations in Europe. Day ski passes run approximately 550 NOK in 2026. Ski and snowboard rental is available on site. Check fonnaresort.no for lift status and current conditions the morning of your visit. Weather on the plateau changes quickly, and lifts close in wind.

Combining a half-day ski with an afternoon glacier walk is feasible if you plan the timings carefully. The ski runs finish early afternoon. Book the afternoon Blue Ice Hike slot through folgefonni.no to match.

Practical information (2026)

  • Operator: Folgefonni Glacier Team (folgefonni.no)
  • Guide shop location: Fonna Glacier Ski Resort, end of toll road above Jondal, 1,199 meters elevation
  • Distance from Jondal: 19 km, 30 to 40 minutes by car
  • Toll road: 120 NOK per car, toll station 8 km above Jondal
  • Jondal to Tørvikbygd ferry: 20 minutes, hourly in summer (norled.no)
  • Panorama Glacier Hike: From 1,190 NOK. 3 to 4 hours. Mid-June to mid-August.
  • Blue Ice Hike: From 1,249 NOK. 5 to 6 hours. End of May to November. Age 10+.
  • Glacier Crossing 2-day: From 1,590 NOK. DNT cabin overnight.
  • Equipment provided: Crampons, ice axe, harness
  • Glacier area: Folgefonna covers approximately 207 km² across three connected ice caps
  • Park status: Folgefonna National Park (established 2005)

The Folgefonna glacier walk is one of the few day-trip glacier experiences left in mainland Norway that starts from a fjord and finishes on live ice within a single afternoon. The operator is experienced, the kit is included, and the route shows you ice that started falling as snow when the Napoleonic Wars were ending. That is a specific experience. Book it in advance.
folgefonna-glacier-walk-report.featuredImageAlt: Hardangerfjord from water level. Folgefonna glacier sits 1,200 meters above these same shores.
folgefonna-glacier-walk-report.metaTitle: Folgefonna Glacier Walk 2026: Blue Ice Hike from Fonna Glacier Ski Resort | NorgeTravel
folgefonna-glacier-walk-report.metaDescription: Guided Folgefonna glacier walks with Folgefonni Glacier Team above Hardangerfjord. Blue Ice Hike from 1,249 NOK, 5 to 6 hours. 19 km drive from Jondal to Fonna Glacier Ski Resort, 120 NOK toll, crampons and ice axe provided.

sognefjellet-rv55-national-tourist-route.title: Driving the Rv55 Sognefjellet 2026: 108 km Lom to Gaupne, summit 1,434 m at Fantesteinen
sognefjellet-rv55-national-tourist-route.subtitle: Northern Europe's highest mountain pass road. Seasonal opening from late May. Official National Tourist Route viewpoints at Nedre Oscarshaug, Mefjellet, Sognefjellshytta, and Turtagrø. Here is the current route logistics and why the timing of your crossing matters.
sognefjellet-rv55-national-tourist-route.excerpt: The Rv55 Sognefjellet runs 108 km between Lom in Gudbrandsdalen and Gaupne on Lustrafjord, summit 1,434 meters at Fantesteinen near Sognefjellshytta. It is a seasonal road, typically open late May to early November. The 2024 season was cancelled after rockfalls. Here is the verified route, viewpoints, and 2026 status check.
sognefjellet-rv55-national-tourist-route.content:
The Rv55 is on every Norwegian National Tourist Route list and appears in every fjord itinerary. The description in older guides is some version of "spectacular mountain scenery." Here is the practical 2026 version: 108 km between Lom and Gaupne, crossing a 1,434-meter plateau at Fantesteinen, with official viewpoints designed by Norwegian architects, one staffed mountain lodge at the highest point, and a Jostedalsbreen outlet glacier accessible via a side road at the western end. Drive time is 2.5 to 3 hours without stops. In clear weather in June it is one of the best drives in Europe. In low cloud and rain it is a narrow road with hairpin bends and limited guardrail. Both are part of the same route.

The route: Lom to Gaupne (or reverse)

The National Tourist Route runs Lom to Gaupne, 108 km. This is the correct endpoint information. Older articles that describe the route as "Sogndal to Lom" are wrong. Sogndal sits on Rv5 and E39, not Rv55. The western terminus of Rv55 is Gaupne, at the head of Lustrafjord. From Gaupne, the road climbs through Jostedalen and over the pass to Lom in Gudbrandsdalen.

Driving west to east (Gaupne to Lom): the road follows Lustrafjord north, then climbs through Skjolden and Fortun into the Jotunheimen massif. The Turtagrø section delivers the first high views toward the Hurrungane range. The summit at Fantesteinen sits near Sognefjellshytta. The descent east into Bøverdalen drops you down to Lom.

Driving east to west (Lom to Gaupne): the ascent from Lom climbs Bøverdalen, crosses Fantesteinen, and drops down the western side through Turtagrø and Fortun to Skjolden and Gaupne. The concentrated mountain scenery sits in the middle third of the drive.

The official viewpoints

Rv55 is part of the Nasjonale turistveger programme, which means the viewpoints are architect-designed pull-outs, not just road-shoulder gravel. Four are official stops in 2026.

  • Nedre Oscarshaug. Architect Carl-Viggo Hølmebakk. A viewing scope identifies the named peaks of the Hurrungane range across the valley. This is the best stop for understanding what you are looking at on the western approach.
  • Oscarshaug. Separate viewpoint nearby, named for King Oscar II, who crossed Sognefjellet in 1860.
  • Mefjellet. Stone sculpture by Knut Wold frames the Fanaråken glacier view.
  • Sognefjellshytta. The rest area at the pass high point, 1,400 meters. Staffed lodge with meals, fuel for people, and an emergency refuge in bad weather.
  • Turtagrø. On-route waypoint, historic hotel, and base for Hurrungane climbing.

Note: Valdresflye is not on Rv55. It is the summit of Fv51, a separate National Tourist Route, further east. Older NorgeTravel copy referencing "Valdresflye viewpoint near Bøvertun" was wrong. Røisheim is also not a viewpoint. It is a historic coaching inn in Bøverdalen, worth a stop but not an official National Tourist Route pull-out.

The Nigardsbreen detour

Nigardsbreen is an outlet arm of Jostedalsbreen, the largest glacier on mainland Europe. It is NOT accessed directly from Rv55. From Gaupne, take Rv604 north into Jostedalen, approximately 30 km to the Breheimsenteret visitor centre. The side trip adds roughly 90 minutes each way plus time at the ice.

Breheimsenteret is open daily from 1 May to 30 September in 2026. From the visitor centre, a short walk (or the small electric boat across Nigardsbrevatnet) brings you to the ice front. Guided ice walks are run by Jostedalen Breførarlag (bfl.no), 1 June to 15 September 2026. The short hike is approximately 4.5 hours including transport. Book ahead in July and August.

When it opens and when it closes

The Rv55 Sognefjellet is a seasonal road. It typically opens in late May and closes in late October or at first sustained snowfall, whichever comes first. The exact opening date varies by year and is determined by the spring snow-clearing operation. Check vegvesen.no before you drive.

Important caveat: the 2024 summer season was cancelled after rockfalls closed the road for the year. Do not assume "Rv55 is always open in July." Confirm current status on vegvesen.no (search the route number and check for real-time notices). The road can close briefly mid-season for maintenance or weather.

What works

  • Clear weather in June. Once the road has opened, June combines remaining snow walls at the summit with clear plateau visibility and significantly less traffic than July. This is the best month for the crossing.
  • Stopping at Nedre Oscarshaug early. The viewing scope helps you identify the peaks you are about to drive past. If you skip it, the rest of the drive loses context.
  • Sognefjellshytta as a break point. The staffed lodge at 1,400 meters is roughly the mid-point of the mountain section. Hot food, shelter if conditions deteriorate.
  • Combining with the Nigardsbreen detour. If you have a full day, Gaupne in the morning, Nigardsbreen ice walk, then the Rv55 climb in the afternoon. Overnight in Lom or Sognefjellshytta.

What does not work

  • Large motorhomes and wide caravans. Rv55 has narrow sections and switchbacks, particularly near Turtagrø. Norway's general maximum vehicle width is 2.55 meters. Rental motorhomes at the upper end of that range are slow and stressful on this road in peak traffic.
  • Assuming the road is open. After the 2024 closure, the lesson is clear: confirm status on vegvesen.no the morning of the crossing.
  • Evening crossings without accommodation arranged. The summit section has limited services. Book Sognefjellshytta, Turtagrø, Lom, or Gaupne ahead in July and August.
  • Trusting phone GPS for the detour to Nigardsbreen. The junction is signed in Gaupne. Follow Rv604 north, not any shortcut the app suggests.

Fuel and services

Fill up in Lom (Circle K) before starting the climb eastbound. Westbound, fuel in Gaupne before continuing up into Jostedalen or over the pass. There is no reliable mid-route fuel station at the summit. Sognefjellshytta provides meals and accommodation, not fuel for vehicles.

Practical information (2026)

  • Route: Rv55 Sognefjellet National Tourist Route, Lom to Gaupne (or reverse)
  • Distance: 108 km
  • Drive time: 2.5 to 3 hours without stops. Allow 4 to 5 hours with viewpoints.
  • Highest point: 1,434 meters at Fantesteinen, near Sognefjellshytta. Northern Europe's highest mountain pass road.
  • Season: Typically late May to late October. Check vegvesen.no for 2026 opening date.
  • 2024 note: Road was closed the entire summer season following rockfalls. Confirm current status before planning around the route.
  • Toll: No road toll
  • Official viewpoints: Nedre Oscarshaug, Oscarshaug, Mefjellet, Sognefjellshytta, Turtagrø
  • Sognefjellshytta: Staffed lodge at 1,400 m with meals and accommodation
  • Nigardsbreen access: Via Rv604 from Gaupne (not directly from Rv55). Breheimsenteret open 1 May to 30 September 2026. Guided ice walks via Jostedalen Breførarlag 1 June to 15 September.
  • Fuel: Lom (Circle K) eastbound. Gaupne westbound. No reliable summit fuel.
  • Source for road status: vegvesen.no
  • Source for National Tourist Route: nasjonaleturistveger.no

The Rv55 is the correct route between Sognefjord and Jotunheimen in summer. It earns its National Tourist Route designation. The two things that matter: confirm the road is actually open the morning you plan to drive, and allow time for the official viewpoints. The road itself is the attraction. Driving it in a hurry defeats the point.
sognefjellet-rv55-national-tourist-route.featuredImageAlt: Sognefjord viewpoint — the fjord that the Rv55 climbs away from on its way to 1,434 meters
sognefjellet-rv55-national-tourist-route.metaTitle: Driving the Rv55 Sognefjellet 2026: Northern Europe's Highest Mountain Pass | NorgeTravel
sognefjellet-rv55-national-tourist-route.metaDescription: Complete driving guide for Rv55 Sognefjellet. 1,434m, open mid-May to late October, 120 km Sogndal to Lom. Opening date logistics, Nigardsbreen glacier detour, fuel planning and viewpoints.

urnes-stave-church-lustrafjord.title: Urnes Stave Church 2026: the oldest stave church in Norway, 20-minute ferry from Solvorn, open 2 May to 30 September
urnes-stave-church-lustrafjord.subtitle: The carved north portal dates to roughly 1070 CE. The current building stands from around 1130. Interior access is guided only. Here is the Lustrabaatane ferry, the 2026 hours, and what the carvings actually show.
urnes-stave-church-lustrafjord.excerpt: Urnes stavkyrkje was built around 1130 CE on the eastern shore of Lustrafjord. Carved panels reused from an earlier church date to approximately 1070. Access is via the 20-minute Solvorn–Ornes ferry run by Lustrabaatane. The 2026 season runs 2 May to 30 September. UNESCO listed since 1979.
urnes-stave-church-lustrafjord.content:
Most Norwegian stave churches are reconstructions, replicas, or heavily restored. Urnes is not. The building standing above Lustrafjord was built around 1130 CE. The carved wooden panels in the north portal, showing the serpentine figures and interlaced animals that give their name to the "Urnes style" of Scandinavian animal ornament, were taken from an earlier church on this site built around 1070 CE. The wood in those panels is roughly 950 years old. The building around them is roughly 895 years old. It is the oldest surviving stave church in Norway.

This is useful to know before you arrive. Without the context you see a small wooden church in a field. With it you stand inside a timber building that predates the unification of the Norwegian kingdom as most history books describe it.

Getting there: the Solvorn–Ornes ferry

Urnes sits on the eastern shore of Lustrafjord, a branch of Sognefjord, in Luster municipality. The practical access is the Solvorn–Ornes ferry run by Lustrabaatane AS, in operation on this crossing since 2001. The crossing takes around 20 minutes. Tickets are sold on board. No pre-booking.

The ferry carries cars as well as foot passengers. Contrary to some older third-party guides, it is not a passenger-only service. Capacity is limited, and in peak season cars may need to wait for a later sailing. If you are on a day trip from Sogndal or Flåm, arriving at Solvorn by 09:30 gives you the best chance of loading a car onto an early crossing.

2026 one-way fares (per Lustrabaatane): adult passenger 70 NOK, child aged 4 to 16 years 35 NOK, car up to 6 meters 200 NOK, car 6 to 7 meters 410 NOK, motorcycle or moped 125 NOK. Summer sailings run roughly hourly from 10:00 to 16:50 with earlier weekday departures from 07:10. Confirm the current timetable at en.lustrabaatane.no the morning of the crossing.

Solvorn is 45 minutes by road from Sogndal via Rv55 along the fjord shore. From Flåm, drive the Aurlandsvegen to Lærdal, then east along E16 and Rv55 to Solvorn, roughly 1 hour 30 minutes. The drive-around alternative to the ferry (via Skjolden at the head of Lustrafjord) is possible but adds substantial time on narrow fjord roads. For a half-day visit, take the ferry.

The church itself

The exterior: a small brown-black timber building on a hillside above the fjord with dragon-head gables visible from the water. Inside: one main nave, original stave posts, the choir, and the reused carved elements from the earlier 11th-century church. The building is modest in size.

Interior access is guided only. Visits run as short guided tours of roughly 20 minutes. Tickets are sold from a small white ticket house a short walk from the church itself. There is no large visitor centre on site.

2026 season: 2 May to 30 September. Closed 17 May (Constitution Day). Daily opening hours during the season are 10:30 to 17:45. Standard adult admission in 2026 is 150 NOK. Senior (Honnør) admission is 130 NOK. Confirm child and family rates at stavechurch.com before you travel.

What the carvings actually show

The north portal panels are the reason the church carries UNESCO status. The carvings show a large quadruped, often described as a lion-like animal, wrestling with serpents, surrounded by interlaced animal bodies biting each other. These motifs define the final phase of Viking-Age animal ornament in Scandinavia, the style art historians now simply call "Urnes." The same visual language appears in metalwork, manuscript illumination, and carved stonework across northern Europe from the late 10th century into the 12th. The church at Urnes is the defining example of the style in timber, and it has been on the UNESCO World Heritage List since 1979.

Confirm interior photography rules with the guide on arrival. Flash and interior photography are commonly restricted in Fortidsminneforeningen properties. Ask before you shoot.

The graveyard and the view

The churchyard contains grave markers spanning several centuries. The slope behind the church faces east along Lustrafjord toward the outlet arms of the Jostedalsbreen ice cap. On a clear day, the Nigardsbreen glacier snout is visible at the head of the valley above Gaupne. A medieval timber church with a direct sightline to the largest glacier in mainland Europe. Both UNESCO listed. Both within 30 km of each other.

What works

  • Arriving by mid-morning. A 10:00 ferry from Solvorn puts you at the church for the first guided tour. Smaller groups, quieter churchyard.
  • Booking the visit as a half-day, not a drive-by. Crossing, walk up, guided tour, walk down, crossing back. Budget three hours from Solvorn car park to Solvorn car park.
  • Combining with Nigardsbreen. If you have a full day, Urnes in the morning and the Nigardsbreen visitor centre in the afternoon is a feasible Lustrafjord loop.
  • Walking up from the Ornes ferry landing. 10 minutes uphill. Good legs, clear signage.

What does not work

  • Relying on outdated ferry information. Guides that say "passenger only" or quote 2010-era fares are out of date. Use en.lustrabaatane.no for current times and prices.
  • Arriving before the season opens. The church is closed before 2 May. The ferry runs year-round but the church interior is not accessible out of season.
  • Planning around a self-guided interior visit. Interior access is guided only. Build time for the tour into your schedule.
  • Visiting on 17 May. Closed for Constitution Day.

Practical information (2026)

  • Location: Ornes, eastern shore of Lustrafjord, Luster municipality, Vestland
  • Owner/operator: Fortidsminneforeningen (National Trust of Norway)
  • UNESCO listed: 1979
  • Season: 2 May to 30 September 2026. Closed 17 May.
  • Daily hours: 10:30 to 17:45
  • Admission 2026: Adult 150 NOK. Senior 130 NOK. Child and family rates at stavechurch.com.
  • Access: Guided tours only, approximately 20 minutes
  • Ferry operator: Lustrabaatane AS (Solvorn–Ornes)
  • Crossing time: Approximately 20 minutes
  • Ferry 2026 one-way fares: Adult 70 NOK, child (4–16) 35 NOK, car up to 6 m 200 NOK, car 6–7 m 410 NOK, motorcycle 125 NOK
  • Timetable and tickets: en.lustrabaatane.no
  • Drive from Sogndal to Solvorn: 45 minutes
  • Drive from Flåm to Solvorn: 1 hour 30 minutes via Aurland and Lærdal
  • Contact: urnes@stavechurch.com, +47 413 94 821

Urnes takes half a day including the ferry from Sogndal. It is worth exactly that time. The church is not large, the crossing is short, and the carved panels on the north portal are the kind of object you remember for reasons that are hard to articulate to someone who has not stood in front of them.
urnes-stave-church-lustrafjord.featuredImageAlt: Urnes Stave Church above Lustrafjord, the oldest surviving stave church in Norway built circa 1130 CE
urnes-stave-church-lustrafjord.metaTitle: Urnes Stave Church Guide 2026: Ferry, Opening Hours, and What to Look For | NorgeTravel
urnes-stave-church-lustrafjord.metaDescription: Complete guide to Urnes Stave Church. 1130 CE, UNESCO World Heritage, ferry from Solvorn. Opening hours, entry price, drive from Sogndal, and what the 950-year-old carved panels actually show.

prest-viewpoint-flam-trail.title: Prest 2026: the 1,478-metre summit above Aurlandsfjord, trailhead on the Bjørgavegen past Stegastein
prest-viewpoint-flam-trail.subtitle: The trail starts from the Bjørgavegen 8 km above Aurland, climbs 500 m to the Prest summit at 1,478 m, and looks straight down onto Aurlandsfjord. 2.1 km each way, DNT Red, mid-June to early October.
prest-viewpoint-flam-trail.excerpt: The Prest trail climbs from a parking bay on the Bjørgavegen to the Prest summit at 1,478 metres, with the Røyrgrind beacon at 1,363 m as an intermediate target. 2.1 km each way, 500 m gain, DNT Red, 2 to 3 hours round trip. Steep, exposed, and rewarded with a vertical sightline down onto Aurlandsfjord.
prest-viewpoint-flam-trail.content:
The Prest trail is not the hike most visitors find when they search for "Flåm hiking." It does not leave from the railway terminus, the campsite, or the ferry pier. It leaves from a small parking bay on the Bjørgavegen, 8 km above Aurlandsvangen, past the Stegastein platform, on the mountain road that climbs the Aurlandsfjellet scenic route.

Most hikers who start this trail drove up from Aurland to photograph Stegastein first. Only a small fraction keep driving past the platform, look for the unmarked lay-by, and climb the 500 metres to the ridge above. That is why this trail stays quiet even in late July.

The trailhead

The Prest trailhead is a small unmarked parking bay on the Bjørgavegen (Fv243), approximately 8 km from Aurlandsvangen and past the Stegastein platform. From Stegastein, continue up the scenic route for roughly 2 km. The parking bay is on the right, with space for four or five cars. A faint path leaves the road and climbs directly up the slope.

There is no signposting at the trailhead. The Norwegian name of the trail is Prest, the summit is sometimes marked on maps as Prestholet. Download the ut.no app route offline before you leave mobile coverage in Aurland. The path is cairned once you are on the slope, but finding the start requires the app or local knowledge.

The route

The trail climbs steadily from the road at approximately 980 m to the Røyrgrind beacon at 1,363 m. This first section takes 1 to 1.5 hours and covers about 1.4 km of walking. The beacon is a stone cairn visible from some distance and is the natural turnaround point for hikers who do not want the full summit. The view from Røyrgrind is already substantial.

From Røyrgrind, the route continues across the high ridge to the Prest summit at 1,478 m. This section is approximately 700 m further and adds 115 m of elevation. It takes 30 to 45 minutes each way. The ridge is exposed. In strong wind or poor visibility, the intermediate beacon at Røyrgrind is the correct turnaround.

Total route: 2.1 km each way, 500 m elevation gain, 2 to 3 hours round trip including time at the summit. DNT grade: Red. The gradient is sustained (500 m in 2.1 km is roughly 24%), the terrain is rocky, and the final ridge is exposed without shelter.

The view

From the Prest summit, Aurlandsfjord sits almost vertically below you. The drop from 1,478 m to sea level is one of the steeper sightlines on any marked trail in the Sognefjord system. Aurland village is visible at the head of the fjord to the south. Flåm sits further south at the end of the Flåm arm. The Stegastein platform is visible below you to the east.

Nærøyfjord is not visible from Prest. The Nærøyfjord arm runs west from the fjord junction at Beitelen, behind the ridge that Prest sits on. Trail guides that claim a Nærøyfjord view from this summit are wrong. For Nærøyfjord from height, the Stalheim–Nåli route above Stalheim on the opposite side of the fjord network is the correct trail.

Season and conditions

The Bjørgavegen is a seasonal road. The scenic route opens in late May and closes in mid-October, weather dependent. Check vegvesen.no for current status before driving up. The Prest trail itself is snow-free from mid-June to early October in a typical year. Outside that window, the upper ridge holds snow and ice.

Weather at 1,478 m is not the weather in Aurland. Temperature at the summit runs 8 to 10°C cooler than the fjord floor. Wind on the exposed ridge is consistently stronger than forecast at the valley. Bring a windproof shell, a warm mid-layer, hiking boots with ankle support, and hands-free gloves for the final ridge in early or late season. Check yr.no for the Aurland forecast and add one category of severity for the summit.

Practical information

  • Distance: 2.1 km each way, 4.2 km round trip
  • Elevation gain: 500 m
  • Summit elevation: Prest 1,478 m. Intermediate Røyrgrind beacon 1,363 m.
  • Time: 2 to 3 hours round trip including summit time
  • DNT grade: Red (demanding)
  • Trailhead: Unmarked lay-by on the Bjørgavegen (Fv243), approximately 8 km from Aurlandsvangen, past Stegastein
  • Season: Mid-June to early October. Bjørgavegen opens late May, closes mid-October.
  • Navigation: Download ut.no offline. No signposting at trailhead.
  • Facilities: None. Nearest toilets at Stegastein platform, 2 km back down the road.
  • Emergency: 112 police and rescue, 113 medical

The Prest trail is the hike for travellers who already know they want effort over access. It is the answer to the question "I have seen Stegastein, what is the next level up?" The 500-metre climb and the ridge exposure mean this is not a casual afternoon. The view from the summit is the reason it is worth the drive past Stegastein and the walk from the lay-by.

Ingrid Solheim is the Fjord Logistics Editor at NorgeTravel. She spent eleven years putting tourists into rental cars in Bergen and watching them drive off with itineraries that were never going to work. Her guides are written so that does not happen to you. She can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.
prest-viewpoint-flam-trail.featuredImageAlt: Sognefjord overview — the fjord system that includes Nærøyfjord and the Prest viewpoint above Flåm
prest-viewpoint-flam-trail.metaTitle: Prest Hike 2026: 1,478m Summit above Aurlandsfjord, Trailhead on Bjørgavegen | NorgeTravel
prest-viewpoint-flam-trail.metaDescription: Trail guide for Prest summit above Aurlandsfjord. 2.1 km each way, 500 m elevation gain, DNT Red. Trailhead on the Bjørgavegen past Stegastein. Mid-June to early October.

norway-in-a-nutshell-bergen-review.title: Norway in a Nutshell 2026 from Bergen: Fjord Tours package, four connections, 10 to 12 hours
norway-in-a-nutshell-bergen-review.subtitle: Bergensbanen to Myrdal, Flåmsbana to Flåm, electric cruise on Future of the Fjords to Gudvangen, bus to Voss, Bergensbanen back. Fjord Tours AS runs the package. 2026 price from around 1,595 NOK.
norway-in-a-nutshell-bergen-review.excerpt: Norway in a Nutshell is a Fjord Tours AS trademark package combining Vy Bergensbanen, Flåmsbana, The Fjords DA electric cruise on Future of the Fjords, and bus between Gudvangen and Voss. 10 to 12 hours from Bergen. 2026 price from approximately 1,595 NOK. Here is the segment-by-segment reality and what to book ahead.
norway-in-a-nutshell-bergen-review.content:
Norway in a Nutshell is a registered trademark of Fjord Tours AS, not a generic route name. The company has operated the package since 1982. In 2026, the package stitches four separate operators into a single booking: Vy on the Bergensbanen, Flåmsbana AS on the Flåm Railway, The Fjords DA on the Nærøyfjord cruise, and a coach operator between Gudvangen and Voss. You can book each segment separately if you prefer. The Fjord Tours package guarantees the connections.

I have run this route more times than I can count, most recently in June 2026 to check the current connection windows. Here is what it involves.

The route, segment by segment

Segment 1: Bergen to Myrdal (Bergensbanen, operated by Vy). Departure from Bergen station. Duration approximately 2 hours. The train climbs to 1,237 metres at Finse, the highest station on the Norwegian rail network and the plateau used for the Hoth exterior shots in The Empire Strikes Back. Myrdal station at 867 metres is the junction with the Flåm Railway.

Segment 2: Myrdal to Flåm (Flåmsbana). The Flåmsbana departs Myrdal within approximately 15 minutes of the Bergensbanen arrival. Duration 55 minutes descending. 20 km of track, 20 tunnels, maximum gradient 5.5%. The Kjosfossen waterfall stop at kilometre 17 is a scheduled 5 minutes on the platform. For the best view on the descent, sit on the right side facing forward.

Segment 3: Flåm to Gudvangen (Nærøyfjord cruise, operated by The Fjords DA). Ferry departs Flåm approximately 30 minutes after the Flåmsbana arrival. Duration 2 hours. This is the central segment of the package. The Fjords DA operates the route with the all-electric Future of the Fjords and its sister ship. 18 km of UNESCO-listed Nærøyfjord narrowing to 250 metres at Beitelen. The open top deck is the correct place for this leg. Bring a windproof shell.

Segment 4: Gudvangen to Voss (coach over Stalheimskleiva). The coach departs Gudvangen shortly after the ferry docks. Duration approximately 1 hour 15 minutes. The route includes Stalheimskleiva, a 1.5 km road with 13 hairpin bends on an 18% gradient. The coach uses this section only in the downhill direction from Stalheim toward Gudvangen as a one-way road, so northbound coaches return via the tunnel. Stalheim Hotel at the top is a listed historic building.

Segment 5: Voss to Bergen (Bergensbanen return, operated by Vy). Duration approximately 1 hour 15 minutes. Multiple departures per hour. Arrival in Bergen typically between 18:00 and 20:00 depending on which outbound departure you booked.

What works

  • The connections. Fjord Tours times the four segments within the package so each one waits for the previous one. If a train is late, the ferry holds. If you book segments independently and miss a connection, the next operator is not obligated to wait. For most travellers, the package is worth the small premium over separate bookings for this reason alone.
  • The ferry direction. Flåm to Gudvangen is the correct direction. The ferry enters Nærøyfjord from the Aurlandsfjord junction and you face the narrowing. The reverse sequence works visually but is less dramatic.
  • The lunch window at Flåm. 25 to 30 minutes between the Flåmsbana arrival and the ferry departure. Flåmsbrygga Restaurant at the harbour serves the fish soup in under 15 minutes. The Ægir brewery next door is the slower option.
  • The 2026 price. From around 1,595 NOK per adult for the full round trip through Fjord Tours. Verify the current price at fjordtours.com before booking. Children and rail-pass holders get different rates.

What does not work

  • Booking in July without advance notice. The package sells out in peak summer. Book 2 to 4 weeks ahead via fjordtours.com or through your Bergen accommodation. The Flåmsbana individual tickets also sell out independently in summer.
  • Treating the Stalheimskleiva coach as transport only. The 13 hairpin bends are the point. Sit on the side that faces outward on the descent and look out the window.
  • Expecting to arrive fresh. 10 to 12 hours of trains, ferries, and coaches with viewpoint stops is a full day. Plan a light dinner in Bergen and an unscheduled morning after.
  • Doing Nutshell on the same day as Bryggen. Some itineraries stack them. They do not fit in the same calendar day. Give each its own slot.

Practical information

  • Operator: Fjord Tours AS (trademark holder). Segments operated by Vy, Flåmsbana AS, The Fjords DA, and coach partners.
  • Price: From approximately 1,595 NOK per adult in 2026. Check fjordtours.com for current rates.
  • Duration: 10 to 12 hours Bergen to Bergen
  • Season: Year-round, though winter schedule differs from summer. Stalheimskleiva coach route seasonal.
  • Departure: Bergen station in the morning. Check the current departure time at fjordtours.com before booking accommodation.
  • Booking: fjordtours.com or through major Bergen hotels
  • What to bring: Windproof layer for the ferry top deck, offline map of the fjord, lunch money or packed food, charged phone for photos

Norway in a Nutshell works because the route is correct. It covers the most compressed version of the Bergensbanen, the Flåmsbana, and the best fjord cruise in Norway within one day. The all-electric ferry on Future of the Fjords makes the Nærøyfjord section quieter and cleaner than it was before 2018. Book the package through Fjord Tours, do it Flåm to Gudvangen, and take the top deck rather than sitting inside.

stegastein-viewpoint-aurland-hike.title: Stegastein 2026: the 650-metre platform above Aurlandsfjord on the Fv243 scenic route
stegastein-viewpoint-aurland-hike.subtitle: 30 metres long, 4 metres wide, projecting from the hillside on the Bjørgavegen (Fv243) scenic route. Saunders and Wilhelmsen designed it. It opened in 2006. Here is when to visit and how to avoid the midday coach queue.
stegastein-viewpoint-aurland-hike.excerpt: Stegastein is a 30 by 4 metre viewing platform on the Fv243 scenic route, sitting 650 metres above Aurlandsfjord. Saunders and Wilhelmsen designed it for the Norwegian Scenic Route programme. Free to visit. The 40-space car park fills between 11:00 and 16:00 in peak season. The shuttle from Flåm is the alternative.
stegastein-viewpoint-aurland-hike.content:
The Stegastein viewpoint gets booked into itineraries the way the Trollstigen gets booked. Clients read a list, they see the photograph, they plug the coordinates into Google Maps, and they arrive in a rental car at 13:00 to find a 40-space car park already full. What the photograph does not show is the coach queue, the tour group on the platform, or the narrow switchback road you share with a Dutch campervan on the way down.

The platform itself is 30 metres long and 4 metres wide, and it projects out from the hillside at 650 metres above Aurlandsfjord. The laminated pine structure ends in a glass wall angled downward so the fjord falls away in front of you without visual break. Todd Saunders and Tommie Wilhelmsen designed it for the Norwegian Scenic Route programme. It opened in 2006. It remains one of the most photographed pieces of contemporary architecture in western Norway.

What this viewpoint actually is

Stegastein sits on the Bjørgavegen, the Fv243 mountain road that climbs from Aurland village at sea level to Aurlandsfjellet at roughly 1,300 metres, then drops toward Lærdal as the Snow Road. The road is open summer only (typically late May to mid-October, snow dependent) and is part of the Nasjonal Turistveg network.

The platform is free to visit. There is no entry fee, no ticket, no booking. What it has is a car park with 40 spaces, which in July and August is routinely full between 11:00 and 16:00. Coaches from Flåm cruise calls are the biggest load. If you arrive in a rental car at midday in peak season, you will queue for parking, you will share the platform with 80 other people, and you will budget 90 minutes for what should have been 30.

How to reach Stegastein

Option 1: Drive the Bjørgavegen. From Aurland village, the Fv243 switchbacks up the mountainside. Approximately 6.5 km and 650 metres of elevation, about 20 minutes in good conditions. The road is narrow with passing places. Caravans and trailers are not recommended. Parking at Stegastein: 40 spaces, free. In peak season, arrive before 10:00 or after 17:00.

Option 2: Take the shuttle from Flåm. A summer shuttle from Flåm operates the run up to Stegastein. Check fjordtours.com or the Flåm Visitor Centre for the current operator and schedule. This is the right choice for cruise passengers without a car and for travellers who do not want to drive the switchbacks.

Option 3: Hike up from Aurland. Local walking routes climb from the Aurland valley floor to the plateau at Prest and Stegastein. These are not engineered tourist paths and signposting is limited. If this appeals, consult ut.no for the current route, load the track offline, and allow a full day for the 650-metre climb and descent. Most visitors drive or take the shuttle.

What works

  • The platform design. Saunders and Wilhelmsen built Stegastein from laminated pine and they built it to disappear. The wooden structure curves out from the hillside and ends in a glass wall angled down toward the fjord. You walk to the end, you look down, and there is nothing between you and the water 650 metres below. The architecture does its job. It gets out of the way of the view.
  • Free access. No entry fee, no ticket, no booking. This is unusual for a photographed viewpoint of this calibre. The cost is parking availability in peak hours, not money.
  • The drive itself. If you do drive, the Bjørgavegen is part of the experience. Hairpin turns with the fjord expanding behind you at each switchback. The road continues past Stegastein to Aurlandsfjellet at roughly 1,300 metres and then drops on the Snow Road to Lærdal. This is one of the best mountain drives in Norway.
  • The shuttle option. For travellers without a car, and for anyone uncomfortable on narrow mountain roads, the summer shuttle from Flåm solves the access problem.

What does not work

  • Peak-season crowding at the platform. Between 11:00 and 16:00 from late June through mid-August, expect 60 to 100 people on the platform at once. The 30 by 4 metre deck is not small, but it is not empty either. The quiet photograph you saw online was taken at 07:00 or 20:00. Plan accordingly.
  • The road closes in winter. The Bjørgavegen and the Aurlandsfjellet scenic route are typically closed from mid-October to late May. If you are visiting between November and April, Stegastein is not on your list.
  • No facilities at the platform. There is a small wooden toilet block at the car park and nothing else. No café, no shop, no bench, no shelter. In rain, you get wet. In cold, you get cold. Bring what you need.
  • Vehicle restrictions. Caravans and trailers are not recommended on the Fv243. The passing places are narrow and the gradient is steep.

The honest assessment

Stegastein is worth visiting. The platform design is genuinely good, the view of Aurlandsfjord is among the best in the Sognefjord system, and the architectural context matters. This is part of the Nasjonal Turistveg programme that commissioned some of Norway's best contemporary infrastructure design.

What is not worth doing is queuing for parking at midday and then queuing to get to the end of the platform. If you are driving, drive early or late. If you are coming off a cruise call in Flåm, take the shuttle rather than a taxi. If you want effort on your day, the Prest summit 8 km further up the Bjørgavegen gives you a DNT Red climb with a higher sightline onto the same fjord.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Platform nameStegastein (Nasjonal Turistveg Aurlandsfjellet)
Elevation650 m above Aurlandsfjord
Platform dimensions30 m long, 4 m wide
ArchitectTodd Saunders and Tommie Wilhelmsen, opened 2006
Drive from Aurland6.5 km on Fv243, around 20 minutes in good conditions
SeasonLate May to mid-October, road snow dependent
Entry feeFree
Parking40 spaces, free. Full 11:00 to 16:00 in peak season.
Vehicle restrictionsCaravans and trailers not recommended on Fv243
Facilities at platformToilet only. No café, no shelter.
Best timeBefore 10:00 or after 17:00 in summer
Shuttle from FlåmSummer season, check fjordtours.com
Emergency112 (police/rescue) or 113 (medical)

Driving the Fv243

The Bjørgavegen is narrow, steep, and has passing places rather than two full lanes in several sections. Drive at a moderate pace. Give way to uphill traffic. Take the viewpoint lay-bys above and below the platform if you want to photograph the switchbacks without blocking the road.

In rain, the road surface is slick. In early or late season, patches of ice remain on the upper sections after a cold night. The road closes in winter. Check vegvesen.no for current status if you are driving in May or October.

Who should visit Stegastein

  • Any traveller with half a day in Aurland or Flåm. The drive up, the platform, and the drive back fill 90 minutes to 2 hours. Combined with a meal in Aurland or a Nærøyfjord cruise, this is a complete afternoon.
  • Cruise passengers on the Flåm shuttle. The shuttle plus 45 minutes at the platform is the right form factor for a short port call.
  • Road trippers continuing to Lærdal. The Aurlandsfjellet scenic route continues past Stegastein over the plateau and drops to Lærdal via the Snow Road. If you are driving east anyway, Stegastein is on the route.

Who should skip Stegastein

  • Anyone visiting between mid-October and late May. The road is closed. The platform is inaccessible. Come back in June.
  • Anyone without at least 90 minutes to spare on the visit. The round trip from Aurland takes that much even in off-peak conditions. Rushing it means driving the switchbacks faster than you should.
  • Anyone who dislikes exposed viewpoints. The platform ends in a glass wall with the fjord 650 metres below. The guardrail is a simple metal bar. If that sounds stressful to read, the platform itself will be stressful.

Ingrid Solheim is the Fjord Logistics Editor at NorgeTravel. She spent eleven years putting tourists into rental cars in Bergen and watching them drive off with itineraries that were never going to work. Her guides are written so that does not happen to you. She can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

bakka-nali-farm-trail-naeroyfjord.title: Stalheim to Nåli 2026: the 500-metre viewpoint above Nærøydalen, 2.8 km each way on DNT Blue
bakka-nali-farm-trail-naeroyfjord.subtitle: The trail leaves from the Stalheim Hotel above Stalheimskleiva, climbs 250 metres through old farm terrain to the Nåli viewpoint, and looks straight down the Nærøydalen valley toward Gudvangen. 2.8 km each way, DNT Blue, 2 to 4 hours round trip.
bakka-nali-farm-trail-naeroyfjord.excerpt: The Stalheim to Nåli trail climbs from the historic Stalheim Hotel above the Stalheimskleiva road to the Nåli viewpoint at roughly 500 metres above sea level. 2.8 km each way, 250 to 260 metres of elevation gain, DNT Blue grade. The view looks down the Nærøydalen valley toward Gudvangen. Note: Bakka to Nåli is not a recognised trail. The correct route starts from Stalheim.
bakka-nali-farm-trail-naeroyfjord.content:
First, the naming. You will find references online to a "Bakka–Nåli" trail on Nærøyfjord. The trail does not exist under that name. Bakka is a village on the western shore of Nærøyfjord. Nåli is a viewpoint above Stalheim, on the ridge at the head of the Nærøydalen valley. They sit on opposite sides of different bodies of water and are not connected by a marked trail. The route that is real, marked, and published on ut.no is the Stalheim–Nåli trail. That is the trail this guide covers.

Stalheim to Nåli is the short, steady climb from the historic Stalheim Hotel above Stalheimskleiva to a viewpoint at roughly 500 metres above sea level with a clean sightline down the Nærøydalen valley. 2.8 km each way, 250 to 260 metres of elevation gain, DNT Blue, and 2 to 4 hours round trip at a walking pace. It is one of the most accessible viewpoint hikes in the Sognefjord hinterland and one of the quietest.

What this trail actually is

The Stalheim–Nåli path leaves from behind the Stalheim Hotel and climbs the slope above the hotel grounds to the Nåli viewpoint. The trail is marked by the local hiking authority and shown on ut.no. The grade is DNT Blue: moderate effort, distances under 10 km, elevation gain under 600 metres, and no technical scrambling.

The route runs through mixed forest and old grazing land. You gain elevation steadily rather than in steep pulses. The final approach opens out of the trees with the Nærøydalen valley stretching below you. On a clear day, the valley floor is visible all the way down toward Gudvangen.

Time: approximately 1 to 2 hours up, 1 to 1.5 hours down. Budget 2 to 4 hours for the round trip including time at the viewpoint.

What you see from Nåli

The viewpoint looks down the Nærøydalen valley, not at Nærøyfjord itself. Nærøydalen is the green, glacially carved valley that drops from Stalheim toward Gudvangen, flanked on both sides by cliff walls that rise 900 to 1,000 metres from the valley floor. The Nærøyelva river runs along the valley floor. The Stalheimskleiva road switchbacks visibly below the hotel.

Nærøyfjord proper begins at Gudvangen, several kilometres down the valley. You do not see the fjord water from Nåli. You see the landscape that leads into it, which is arguably the more complete Nærøy view: the glacial geology that created the narrowest UNESCO fjord in Europe, framed in a single sightline.

What works

  • The grade. DNT Blue means this hike is accessible to most travellers with reasonable fitness. No scrambling, no exposure. 250 metres of elevation on a marked path is a normal afternoon walk, not an expedition.
  • The trailhead access. The Stalheim Hotel sits directly on the E16 above the Stalheimskleiva, with parking on site. If you are driving between Voss and Gudvangen, the trailhead is a 2-minute detour.
  • The valley perspective. The view down Nærøydalen toward Gudvangen shows the landscape that the Norway in a Nutshell ferry passengers only see from sea level. The scale of the valley cliffs is different from above. This is the complement to the fjord cruise, not a replacement.
  • The quiet. The Stalheim Hotel sees passing traffic but almost no one walks up to Nåli. You will likely have the viewpoint to yourself outside peak summer weekends.

What does not work

  • Expecting a Nærøyfjord water view. There is no fjord water visible from Nåli. The view is of the valley that leads to the fjord. If you want water, book the Flåm to Gudvangen cruise.
  • Winter access. Snow on the upper section of the trail closes it for casual walkers from November through April. Stalheimskleiva road is closed in winter. Come in May to October.
  • No facilities on the trail. Stalheim Hotel has food, toilets, and water. The trail itself has none of the above. Bring water and a snack.
  • Confusion with "Bakka–Nåli." This is not a trail. If you are using a guide that claims a Bakka to Nåli route with an 800-metre climb from the fjord shore, the guide is wrong. The correct trail starts at Stalheim.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Trail nameStalheim to Nåli (marked on ut.no)
Distance2.8 km each way, 5.6 km round trip
Elevation gain250 to 260 m
Viewpoint elevationApproximately 500 m above sea level
Time2 to 4 hours round trip
GradeDNT Blue (moderate)
SeasonMay to October
Trail feeFree
TrailheadStalheim Hotel, above Stalheimskleiva, E16 between Voss and Gudvangen
ParkingStalheim Hotel car park
FacilitiesAt Stalheim Hotel. None on trail.
NavigationDownload ut.no route offline before starting
Emergency112 police and rescue, 113 medical

Trail safety

Stalheim to Nåli is DNT Blue and does not carry the exposure or technical risk of the Red-graded fjord trails. Three Fjellvettreglene rules still apply:

  • Rule 1: Plan and communicate your route. Tell reception at Stalheim Hotel or your accommodation that you are walking to Nåli. Download the ut.no route offline before you leave mobile coverage.
  • Rule 2: Adapt the trip to ability and conditions. 250 metres of elevation in 2.8 km is a moderate walking grade. Hiking shoes rather than trainers. A rain shell. Water.
  • Rule 3: Pay attention to weather and avalanche forecasts. Check yr.no for Stalheim before you start. The viewpoint has no shelter. In heavy rain or wind, the forested lower section is workable but the exposed upper ridge is not.

Who should hike this

  • Travellers with a spare afternoon in the Voss to Gudvangen corridor. If you are driving between Bergen and Flåm on the E16, Stalheim to Nåli adds 3 to 4 hours and gives you the valley view that the road only hints at.
  • Families with older children (10+). The grade is manageable and the viewpoint is a genuine reward. This works as a first Norwegian hike for visitors with moderate fitness.
  • Travellers doing Norway in a Nutshell who have an extra day. The cruise shows you Nærøyfjord from the water. Nåli shows you the valley that leads to it from 500 metres above.

Who should skip this

  • Visitors between November and April. Trail and road conditions do not support casual hiking. Come back in May.
  • Anyone expecting a view of Nærøyfjord water. The fjord is not visible from Nåli. If the water view is what you want, the Prest summit above Aurland is the better choice, or book the Flåm to Gudvangen cruise.
  • Travellers with under 3 hours to spare. The round trip is 2 to 4 hours depending on pace and time at the viewpoint. A 60-minute tourist stop does not cover it.

Ingrid Solheim is the Fjord Logistics Editor at NorgeTravel. She spent eleven years putting tourists into rental cars in Bergen and watching them drive off with itineraries that were never going to work. Her guides are written so that does not happen to you. She can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

bakka-nali-farm-trail-naeroyfjord.metaTitle: Stalheim to Nåli 2026: 500m Viewpoint above Nærøydalen, DNT Blue | NorgeTravel
bakka-nali-farm-trail-naeroyfjord.metaDescription: Stalheim to Nåli trail guide. 2.8 km each way, 250 m gain, DNT Blue, 2 to 4 hours round trip. Trailhead at Stalheim Hotel on E16. Looks down Nærøydalen toward Gudvangen. Note: Bakka to Nåli is not a recognised trail.

sherpatrappa-floya-tromso.title: Sherpatrappa to Fløya: 1,200 stone steps above Tromsø
sherpatrappa-floya-tromso.subtitle: The Nepalese-built staircase climbs 421 meters from Tromsdalen to the Storsteinen plateau, with Fløya (671m) another 45 minutes above. The most-walked mountain path in the Arctic.
sherpatrappa-floya-tromso.excerpt: Sherpatrappa is a 1,200-step stone staircase built by Nepalese sherpas between 2015 and 2019. It climbs from the Fjellheisen cable car base in Tromsdalen to the Storsteinen viewpoint at 421m, with an onward ridge to Fløya at 671m. Free, year-round (with winter caveats), and the single fastest way to put Tromsø below your boots.
sherpatrappa-floya-tromso.content:
Every visitor to Tromsø looks up at the same mountain. Fløyfjellet sits directly across Tromsøysundet from the city centre, with the Fjellheisen cable car line running up its western face. Until 2015, the only way onto that mountain on foot was a rough path through birch forest and scree. Then the Nepalese sherpas arrived.

Sherpatrappa (the Sherpa Stairs) opened in sections between 2015 and 2019. Fifteen Nepalese stone-workers, most of them trained on the Himalayan trekking paths of their home country, cut and set roughly 1,200 granite steps up the steepest face of the mountain. The project was commissioned by Ishavskysten Friluftsråd (the regional outdoor council) and funded by Troms county, Tromsø municipality, and the Norwegian Environment Directorate. The total cost ran to approximately 14 million NOK across four construction seasons.

What they built is the most-walked mountain path in Nord-Norge. On a clear Saturday in July, counters have registered over 2,000 ascents in a single day. The stairs have a known failure mode for underprepared visitors: a step count that looks manageable on paper and an elevation profile that separates the fit from the optimistic within the first 200 steps.

What this trail actually is

The path is a staircase. Not a metaphorical one. Granite blocks, cut on site and set into the hillside, forming a continuous stepped route from approximately 20m at Solliveien in Tromsdalen to 421m at the Fjellheisen upper station (Storsteinen). The elevation gain is 400 meters over roughly 2 kilometers of stair length.

From Storsteinen, a well-worn footpath continues north along the ridge to Fløya, the actual summit of Fløyfjellet at 671m. This section is not sherpa-built. It is an earth-and-rock trail across alpine tundra with exposed sections above 500m. The total ascent from Tromsdalen to Fløya is 650m over about 3 km.

Most people stop at Storsteinen, take the panoramic photograph over Tromsø, and ride the Fjellheisen cable car back down. Some continue to Fløya. Very few do both ways on foot.

Three routes up the mountain

Option 1: Sherpa Stairs up, cable car down. 1.5 to 2.5 hours of climbing, 8 to 10 minutes of descent. Buy a one-way Fjellheisen ticket (NOK 185 adult) at the upper station. Cars run every half-hour, extended to evening in aurora season. The easiest knee-friendly plan.

Option 2: Full round trip on foot. 3 to 4 hours, 800m of cumulative elevation change. The descent on the granite steps is harder than the ascent. Trekking poles help. Not recommended for anyone with prior knee or ankle problems.

Option 3: Stairs to Storsteinen, continue to Fløya. Add 45 minutes each way from Storsteinen to the Fløya summit ridge. 4 to 5 hours total. The summit view opens north toward Lyngen Alps and east over Ullsfjord. The upper ridge is exposed to wind and weather changes fast.

What works

  • The build quality. The Sherpa stonework is solid. Most steps are cut from single blocks of local granite, set with drainage channels that prevent ice buildup for most of the shoulder season. The Miljødirektoratet assessment in 2023 rated the structural condition as good with minor maintenance needs concentrated in the upper third.
  • The access. The trailhead is a 10-minute drive or a 20-minute bus ride (route 26) from Tromsø city centre. Parking at the Fjellheisen base costs 50 NOK for 4 hours. The path is 50 meters from the cable car ticket office. No wilderness approach.
  • The year-round potential. Summer access is straightforward. Winter is a different calculation. When snow covers the steps (typically late October to April), the route becomes an ice-climb requiring crampons or microspikes plus poles. Locals do walk the stairs in winter, but the Fjellheisen operator posts daily condition warnings, and rescue callouts for underprepared visitors spike between November and February.
  • The view threshold. Storsteinen sits at exactly the altitude where Tromsø lays itself out clearly below: the island, the mainland, the Arctic Cathedral, Tromsøysundet bridge. Fløya is taller but further back. For most visitors, the Storsteinen viewpoint is the photograph they came for.

What does not work

  • The first 300 steps. The gradient is steeper on the lower section than the upper, and many visitors underestimate it. The stone spacing is regular but tall. Legs burn within 10 minutes. Turning back is a valid decision. Nobody posts a photograph of the parking lot.
  • Winter conditions on the unimproved upper ridge. Fløya sits above the treeline and above the sherpa-built section. Between November and May the ridge can hold hard ice, blown-in snow, and whiteout conditions that appear without warning. Two rescue operations per year on this section is typical. If the cloud base is below 600m, Fløya is off the menu.
  • The cable car crowds. Fjellheisen moves up to 4,500 people per day in July. Storsteinen viewpoint is a queue between 11:00 and 15:00. The stairs are an escape from this. Time your ascent to arrive before 10:00 or after 17:00 and the summit is yours.
  • The descent on wet stone. Rain makes the granite slippery, particularly on the polished upper sections. The handrail runs only intermittently. Wet descents are the most common injury mechanism. If the forecast is rain, go up on the stairs and down on the cable car.

The honest assessment

Sherpatrappa is not a wilderness hike. It is a piece of outdoor infrastructure carrying over 150,000 ascents per year into alpine terrain that did not previously support that volume. The value is the access: 400m of elevation in 90 minutes, a panoramic Arctic city view from a functional mountain summit, and no guide required.

For first-time Tromsø visitors with two half-days, this is the trail. For anyone who wants to say they walked up a Norwegian mountain in an afternoon, this is the trail. For hikers looking for solitude in alpine terrain, it is not the trail.

The extension to Fløya takes this from a tourist staircase into a genuine summit day. Most visitors do not make that extension. The ones who do return with a different photograph than the one everyone shares from Storsteinen.

The logistics

DetailInformation
TrailheadSolliveien 12, Tromsdalen (Fjellheisen lower station)
Distance (stairs to Storsteinen)~2 km one way, approx. 1,200 steps
Elevation to Storsteinen400 m (from 20 m to 421 m)
Continuation to Fløya summit+1 km, +250 m (total 671 m)
Time up (stairs only)1.5–2.5 hrs depending on fitness
DNT gradeBlue (medium). Fløya extension: Red (strenuous) above the treeline
Trail feeFree
Fjellheisen one-wayNOK 185 adult, NOK 95 child (4–15)
Fjellheisen returnNOK 295 adult, NOK 150 child
Parking (lower station)NOK 50 for 4 hours
Bus from centreRoute 26, 20 minutes from Sentrum
SeasonYear-round. Winter (Nov–Apr): microspikes essential
Trailhead GPS69.6490°N, 18.9920°E
Emergency112 (police/rescue) or 113 (medical)

Trail safety

The Fjellvettreglene (Mountain Code) has nine rules. Four of them apply directly to Sherpatrappa:

  • Rule 2: Adapt the trip to ability and conditions. The stairs are DNT Blue. The Fløya extension is Red. The two halves are not the same hike. Do not commit to the ridge if conditions have changed by the time you reach Storsteinen.
  • Rule 3: Pay attention to weather. The summit of Fløya sits above the treeline and is the first thing weather hits when a front crosses from the Norwegian Sea. yr.no gives a reliable 6-hour forecast. Check it at the upper station before committing to the ridge.
  • Rule 4: Be equipped to help yourself. Shell layer, hat, gloves, water, headlamp if you are walking in shoulder season when daylight ends fast. Mobile coverage is continuous on the stairs and intermittent on the ridge.
  • Rule 8: Turn back in time. Storsteinen is a valid destination. If the ridge is in cloud or the wind is above 15 m/s, take the cable car down and come back another day.

Who should walk this

  • Fit visitors with a half-day. The stairs fit into an afternoon slot between tours. Up on foot, down on the cable car, back in the city by dinner.
  • Photographers waiting for aurora. Storsteinen is a working aurora viewpoint on clear winter nights. The cable car runs to 23:00 in season. The walk up at 19:00 in February, when the last light hits the ridge, is worth the cold.
  • Runners. Local trail runners use the stairs for vertical training. Fastest-known-time up is under 22 minutes. This is not a goal for most visitors, but knowing it exists helps calibrate the average 90-minute ascent.

Who should skip this

  • Anyone with knee or ankle problems. Long steep stairs are harder on joints than equivalent trail hiking. If you cannot manage 1,200 uneven steps without pain, ride the cable car and save your knees for flat terrain.
  • Winter visitors without microspikes. Between November and April the stairs can hold packed ice for weeks at a time. Regular hiking boots are insufficient. Microspikes are available to rent in Tromsø from NOK 150 per day at outdoor shops like Verdens Beste Butikk.
  • Visitors on a tight tour schedule. Budget 3 hours minimum for the round trip including cable car. If your cruise ship gives you 4 hours in port, pick the cable car return instead of the stairs.

Bjørn Haugen is the Arctic Field Editor at NorgeTravel. He has guided in Nord-Norge and Svalbard for over twenty years and volunteered with Arctic search and rescue. He writes about the Arctic so underprepared travellers do not become statistics. He can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

Source data: Ishavskysten Friluftsråd project reports (2015–2019); Miljødirektoratet trail assessment 2023; Fjellheisen operator data 2024; Troms and Finnmark county visitor statistics.

tromsdalstinden-summit-tromso.title: Tromsdalstinden: the 1,238m summit that watches over Tromsø
tromsdalstinden-summit-tromso.subtitle: The triangular peak visible from every window in Tromsø is a full-day DNT Red alpine hike. 18 km, 1,100m gain, 8–10 hours on the boot, summer-only.
tromsdalstinden-summit-tromso.excerpt: Tromsdalstinden, or 'Tromsdalstind' locally, is the defining summit of the Tromsø skyline at 1,238m. The standard route from Tverrelvdalen runs 18 km round trip over boulder fields and a steep summit block. DNT Red. Summer-only. Not a trail for a first Arctic hike.
tromsdalstinden-summit-tromso.content:
Every photograph of Tromsø has one detail in common: a triangular peak rising behind the city from the east. That peak is Tromsdalstinden, known locally as Tromsdalstind or simply Tinden. It is 1,238 meters high, it is visible from every street in the city centre on a clear day, and it is the mountain every experienced Tromsø hiker points to when asked for a serious day out.

I have walked this trail roughly thirty times over twenty-five years of guiding in Troms. Half of those ascents ended at the summit. The rest turned back in cloud, wind, or the wrong kind of snow. This is not a trail you complete on every attempt. It is a trail where turning back is often the correct decision, and the locals respect the mountain enough to know that.

What this trail actually is

Tromsdalstinden is a DNT Red alpine hike with the standard route starting at the Tverrelvdalen parking area, approximately 30 minutes' drive east of Tromsø city centre. The trail runs through birch forest for the first 3 km, breaks above the treeline around 400m, and then ascends a broad open slope of heather, rock, and scree toward the summit ridge.

From the end of the approach valley at roughly 700m, the climb steepens. The upper 500m is a mixture of large boulders, patches of permanent snow (even in August), and a defined summit block that requires scrambling on the final 150m. There is no technical climbing gear required, but there is no marked trail above 900m. Route-finding across the boulder field becomes the main challenge when visibility drops.

Round trip is approximately 18 km. Elevation gain is 1,100 meters from the trailhead to the summit. Time on foot is 8 to 10 hours for a typical fit hiker. Experienced parties move faster. Tired parties take longer. Plan for a full day.

Two approaches, neither easy

Standard route from Tverrelvdalen (south). Park at the Tverrelvdalen road end. Follow the marked trail along the valley for 5 km, cross the river on the footbridge, and climb the south slope to the summit ridge. This is the most-used route and the one described in local guidebooks. 18 km round trip, 8–10 hours.

Northern route from Breivikeidet. A longer approach from the Breivikeidet valley north of the mountain. More wilderness character, less traffic, steeper summit approach. 22 km round trip, 10–12 hours. Recommended only for parties comfortable with off-trail navigation in alpine terrain.

Most people combine the ascent with an overnight at Skarvassbu DNT cabin (self-service, 15 bunks) if they are doing the northern route or making a two-day trip. The cabin sits at roughly 800m and requires a DNT membership to access the key system.

What works

  • The view from the top. On a clear summit day, Tromsdalstinden gives you the full panorama of Troms and beyond: the Lyngen Alps to the east, the Vesterålen islands to the west, and Tromsø laid out below with the bridge visible between the city island and the mainland. On the clearest days, the horizon reaches 180 km. This is the landscape photograph that justifies the ten hours.
  • The wilderness character. Tromsdalstinden is inside the city limits of Tromsø, but the upper half of the mountain feels remote. Between the treeline and the summit, you may not see another party. The trail is well-known but not overrun. Even on a peak July Saturday, count 20 to 30 summit attempts, not 200.
  • The season window. The reliable hiking window is late June through mid-September. July and August are the months when the summit block is typically clear of winter snow. Early October sometimes gives the crispest summit days of the year, with the first dustings of new snow on the upper mountain and clearer air than July.

What does not work

  • The weather. Tromsdalstinden is exposed on three sides and catches every front that crosses from the Norwegian Sea. A clear valley morning can turn to summit whiteout in two hours. The Norwegian Meteorological Institute runs a dedicated forecast for peaks in Troms at yr.no — check the 1,200m level, not the Tromsø city forecast. Two of my three turned-back attempts over twenty years were due to weather arriving faster than predicted.
  • The boulder field. The upper 500m is not a trail. It is a boulder field with cairns placed every 30 to 50 meters on the most-used line. In cloud, the cairns disappear. A GPS track saved on your phone is essential — a printed 1:50,000 topo map is better. Compass bearings from known waypoints are the backup.
  • The descent. The boulder field is harder on the descent than the ascent. Loose rock moves unexpectedly. The 1,100m elevation loss takes 4 to 5 hours on tired legs. Trekking poles are not optional for most parties; they are the difference between a manageable descent and an injured one.
  • Summer snow. Snow patches persist on the northern aspects of the summit ridge into early August in most years. They are not avalanche-relevant in high summer, but they are slippery, and a slide on the wrong patch ends in rock at the bottom. Microspikes in the pack are sensible equipment for any ascent before mid-July.

The honest assessment

Tromsdalstinden is the serious day hike of the Tromsø area. It is the mountain local hikers climb once a year to check their fitness. It is not a first Arctic hike, and it is not a substitute for an organized summit trip. I see five or six underprepared attempts per season: visitors in city boots, without a shell layer, without a GPS, attempting the summit in cloud. Most of them turn back before the boulder field. The ones who don't are the ones who keep the local Røde Kors Hjelpekorps busy.

For fit hikers with alpine experience, proper gear, and a clear weather window, it is one of the best summit days in Nord-Norge. For anyone else — including most Tromsø visitors — the Sherpa Stairs to Fløya offers a mountain summit without the full commitment.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Summit elevation1,238 m
Standard trailheadTverrelvdalen road end, 30 min drive from Tromsø centre
Round trip distance~18 km
Elevation gain~1,100 m
Time on foot8–10 hours fit, 10–12 hours moderate
DNT gradeRed (strenuous alpine). Scrambling on the final 150 m
SeasonLate June to mid-September
Trail feeFree
Parking (Tverrelvdalen)Free, limited to ~15 cars
Nearest DNT cabinSkarvassbu (self-service, 15 bunks, key from DNT Tromsø)
Mobile coverageIntermittent above 800 m
Summit GPS69.6408°N, 19.2500°E
Emergency112 (rescue). Local SAR: Tromsø Røde Kors Hjelpekorps

Required equipment

Non-negotiable items for a summit attempt:

  • Hiking boots with ankle support and grip (not trail runners)
  • Hardshell jacket and overtrousers — the summit is exposed and the weather changes fast
  • Insulating layer: fleece or synthetic, plus hat and gloves even in July
  • 1:50,000 topo map of the Tromsdalen area plus compass, with a GPS track as backup (not as primary)
  • Headlamp with spare batteries
  • 2 liters of water minimum, plus food for 10 hours
  • First aid kit, emergency bivvy, whistle
  • Trekking poles (not optional for most parties)
  • Microspikes if hiking before mid-July

Fjellvettreglene (applied)

All nine Mountain Code rules apply to Tromsdalstinden, but five are specifically load-bearing on this trail:

  • Rule 1: Plan your trip and communicate. Tell someone your route and expected return time. The standard route is long enough that phone battery can fail before you are back in coverage.
  • Rule 2: Adapt the trip to ability. DNT Red means strenuous alpine. If you have never done 1,000m of gain in a day on alpine terrain, build up on smaller mountains first. Rødtinden on Kvaløya (644m, Blue) is a good test.
  • Rule 3: Pay attention to weather. Forecasts for Tromsdalstinden come in layers: city forecast (unreliable for the summit), 1,000m forecast (directly relevant), and cloud-base prediction. Abort the summit if the cloud base is below 900m.
  • Rule 7: Use a map and compass. Your phone is a secondary tool. The summit block in cloud has disoriented experienced hikers. GPS coordinates of the summit and the start of the boulder field should be written on paper as well as on the device.
  • Rule 8: Turn back in time. The summit will be there next year. On 30 ascents I have turned back on 15. Nobody regrets a safe descent. A forced overnight on the mountain is a rescue event.

Who should hike this

  • Fit hikers with alpine experience, proper equipment, and a full day
  • Visitors spending a week or more in Tromsø during the July–August window, willing to wait for weather
  • Trail runners and mountaineers using Tromsø as a summer basecamp

Who should skip this

  • First-time Arctic visitors without alpine hiking experience
  • Anyone without proper boots, shell layer, or navigation
  • Cruise ship passengers or anyone on a tight schedule
  • Winter visitors: this is a summer trail. The winter ski ascent is for experienced ski-mountaineers with avalanche training only

Bjørn Haugen is the Arctic Field Editor at NorgeTravel. DNT-certified guide (25+ years), Tromsø Røde Kors Hjelpekorps alumnus. He can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

Source data: DNT Tromsø trail database; Norges geologiske undersøkelse (NGU) topographic survey; Norwegian Meteorological Institute (yr.no) summit forecast archive; Tromsø Røde Kors Hjelpekorps incident reports 2019–2024.
