festvagtind-henningsvaer-hike.title: Festvågtind: the 541-metre peak above Henningsvær
festvagtind-henningsvaer-hike.subtitle: A short, steep DNT Blue with a summit scramble and the best view of Henningsvær's island cluster. Three kilometres total, 500 metres up, two hours for anyone with steady legs.
festvagtind-henningsvaer-hike.excerpt: Festvågtind sits 541 metres directly above Henningsvær on Austvågøya. 3 km round trip, 500 metres of elevation gain, 2 to 3 hours. DNT Blue with a short scramble near the summit. Trailhead on the Rv816 before the Henningsvær bridges. The quieter alternative to Reinebringen.
festvagtind-henningsvaer-hike.content:
The football pitch photo of Henningsvær, the one that shows the pitch on a rock in the middle of the sea with a mountain behind it, is taken from Festvågtind. The mountain is 541 metres high, the trail to the top is 1.5 kilometres each way, and you can be on the summit two hours after you leave your car. The reason more visitors do not do this hike is that Reinebringen is more famous. That is also the reason to do this one.

Festvågtind is DNT Blue with a short summit scramble. It gains 500 metres over 1.5 km ascending, averaging 33 per cent gradient. The path is marked, mostly on rock and short grass, with a genuine but short scramble in the last 50 metres to the summit. Most hikers complete the round trip in 2 to 3 hours. Fit groups in 90 minutes.

The honest grade

Festvågtind is DNT Blue: medium difficulty, elevation under 600 metres, distance under 10 km. The upper summit scramble is short enough that it does not push the route to Red, but it is real. Hands come out of pockets for the last 50 metres. The rock is generally dry, solid granite with clear holds. No chains, no fixed ropes, no exposure to major drops below the ridge line.

The gradient is the main test. 500 metres of gain in 1.5 km is as steep as Reinebringen on average, but without the stone staircase. The surface is a mix of rock slabs, short grass, and a rough-engineered boot-stamp path. Waterproof boots with grip are the right choice. Trail runners work in dry conditions.

The summit has a narrow rocky platform with a cairn. The drops on the west side (toward Henningsvær) are substantial but not exposed at the cairn itself. Stay back from the edge. People have been injured on similar exposed cairns across Norway by approaching the drop for a photograph.

The route

Trailhead. The marked path starts from a small gravel lay-by on the Rv816, approximately 2 kilometres north of Henningsvær, just before the first of the two bridges that connect the village to Austvågøya. Parking is limited: 10 to 15 spaces. In July and August it fills by 10:00. Do not park on the shoulder of the Rv816. The road is narrow and local residents will call tow trucks.

Lower section. The path climbs directly from the roadside through birch scrub and onto the first grass bench at around 200 metres. This section is steady, well-trodden, and unmistakable. 30 to 40 minutes.

Middle section. The grass bench gives way to exposed rock slabs with occasional scree. The gradient increases. Route markers (cairns and red paint) continue to the sub-summit ridge at around 480 metres. 30 to 40 minutes.

Summit scramble. The final 50 metres require hands-on movement over solid granite. No exposure to sheer drops in the standard line. Pick your holds deliberately. The cairn sits on a platform with views over Henningsvær, the Vestfjord, and on a clear day the Lofoten Wall from Austvågøya to Moskenesøya. 10 to 20 minutes.

The view

The summit of Festvågtind sits directly over Henningsvær. The perspective is different from every other Lofoten viewpoint. Most of them look at mountains from across the sea. This one looks down at islands and a working harbour from directly above. The football pitch, the fish-drying racks on Heimøya, the Svingen harbour boats, and the 10 inhabited islets of the village are all visible as a layout rather than a horizon.

On a clear day you can see as far as Austvågøya's east coast and the beginning of Vågakallen's ridge to the south. The light in July is consistent from mid-morning through early afternoon. The light in February is brief but extraordinary when it comes.

The turn-back rule

  • Wet summit scramble. The upper 50 metres are more committing on wet rock. If the last rainfall was in the previous two hours, stop at the sub-summit ridge. The view from there is already good.
  • Wind above 12 m/s. The summit platform is exposed. Not fatally so, but uncomfortable and unstable for photography.
  • Ice or snow above 400 metres. The upper scramble without grip is a different problem than the summer version. Add microspikes or reroute to a lower hike.

Gear for this route

  • Hiking boots with ankle support and grip. The mix of grass, slab, and scramble does not reward flexible trail runners in the wet.
  • Wind shell jacket. The summit has no shelter.
  • Water (1 to 1.5 litres). No water source on route.
  • Snack for the summit. You will want to stay at the cairn longer than you expect.
  • Charged phone. Mobile coverage is reliable across the route.
  • Trekking poles are useful on the descent, particularly if rain made the grass slick.

Fjellvettreglene on this route

Three rules of the Norwegian Mountain Code are directly load-bearing on Festvågtind:

  • Rule 2: Adapt the trip to ability and conditions. The DNT Blue grade assumes dry weather. Upgrade your gear thinking if the forecast shows rain.
  • Rule 4: Be equipped to help yourself and others. Short as the route is, a slip on the summit scramble can mean broken bones. Basic first aid kit applies.
  • Rule 8: Turn back in time. The summit scramble in wet rock is where the turnarounds happen. Do it.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Summit elevation541 m
Distance3 km round trip
Elevation gain500 m
Time2 to 3 hours round trip
DNT gradeBlue (medium) with short summit scramble
SeasonJune through September. Snow above 400m in shoulder months.
TrailheadRv816, 2 km north of Henningsvær, before the first bridge
ParkingSmall gravel lay-by, 10 to 15 spaces, free
Trailhead GPS68.1625°N, 14.1890°E
Mobile coverageAvailable along entire route
Water on routeNone. Carry 1 to 1.5 litres.
Emergency112 (police/rescue), 113 (medical)

Who should hike this

  • Travellers basing in Henningsvær. The trailhead is a 10-minute drive from the village. You can hike before breakfast and be back for coffee at Klatrekafeen.
  • Hikers avoiding the Reinebringen queue. Same quality of summit experience, a genuinely different view, and substantially fewer people.
  • Visitors with limited time in Lofoten. If you have one free morning and you are on Austvågøya, this is the route. Moskenesøya peaks require the full E10 drive.

Who should skip this

  • Anyone uncomfortable with hands-on scrambling. The final 50 metres to the cairn require it. The sub-summit ridge is a good turn-around point with a partial view.
  • Travellers in wet or icy conditions. The summit scramble in rain is a different hike. Wait for a dry window.
  • Hikers with more than half a day. Festvågtind is a morning or an afternoon, not a full day. If you have longer, link it with a kayak paddle in the Henningsvær archipelago or the drive to Svolvær.

Marte Åsheim is the Mountain Safety Editor at NorgeTravel. DNT-certified guide with 18 years of experience across Jotunheimen, Rondane, and the Nordic peaks. She writes trail guides so that you come home afterward. She can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

rornestinden-lyngen-ridge-hike.title: Rørnestinden: the ridge hike above the Kjosen valley, Lyngen Alps
rornestinden-lyngen-ridge-hike.subtitle: A DNT Red ridge climb on the non-glaciated side of the Lyngen peninsula. 900 metres of gain, a steep final ridge with exposure, and a summit view straight across the Lyngenfjord.
rornestinden-lyngen-ridge-hike.excerpt: Rørnestinden is 992 metres, directly above the Kjosen valley in the Lyngen Alps. 8 km round trip, 900 m of gain, 5 to 6 hours. DNT Red with an exposed final ridge. Non-glaciated, so no rope team, but this is an alpine day with serious turn-back conditions.
rornestinden-lyngen-ridge-hike.content:
Rørnestinden is one of the non-glaciated peaks that built Lyngen's reputation with intermediate ski tourers and summer ridge hikers. At 992 metres it is not the highest in the range, but the final 200-metre ridge is exposed, the approach is steep, and the summit looks straight across the Lyngenfjord at the big glaciated faces on the other side. It is a full alpine day without the glacier gear requirement.

This is DNT Red. It is not Besseggen Red. The scrambling is shorter but the ridge has real consequence. If you have not spent time on exposed ridges before, do Store Kjostind's southwest flank first and build up.

The honest grade

Rørnestinden is DNT Red: strenuous, with scrambling and exposure. In summer it is a hands-on ridge, not a hike. The ridge itself is narrow in places. There are no fixed chains, no marked bolts. If cloud closes on the ridge, navigation gets difficult fast because the sides drop steeply on both flanks.

In ski-touring season, Rørnestinden is a committing objective. The approach slope from the Kjosen valley is 35 to 40 degrees in places. This is avalanche terrain. Bring a transceiver, shovel, and probe, and know how to use them under pressure. Avalanche Level 2 skills are the baseline for anyone touring here unguided.

The route (summer)

Trailhead. Park at the small gravel area signed for Rørnestinden on the E6 between Oteren and Skibotn at the mouth of the Kjosen valley. Look for the marked path. Limited parking, 8 to 10 cars.

Lower section. The trail climbs steeply through birch forest for 300 metres of gain, then emerges above the tree line onto open tundra. 60 to 80 minutes.

Middle section. Open slope climbing across scree and grass toward the summit ridge. Marker cairns visible in clear weather. Gradient increases steadily. 60 to 90 minutes.

Summit ridge. The final 200 metres of elevation gain happens on a narrow ridge with scrambling and exposure on both sides. Hands come out of pockets. Do not rush. The rock is generally solid granite with clear holds. 45 to 60 minutes.

The turn-back rule

  • Cloud on the ridge. Navigation on the summit ridge in poor visibility is a different problem than in clear weather. If cloud is down to 800 metres, turn back at the sub-summit.
  • Wind above 15 m/s. The ridge is too narrow to be safe at that wind speed. Descend.
  • Wet rock on the ridge. The scrambling sections are slippery when wet. Allow rock to dry before committing to the upper ridge.
  • Rising snow-line in late season. Fresh snow on the summer ridge changes the route to winter conditions. Reverse.

Gear for this route (summer)

  • Alpine hiking boots with stiff sole. Trail runners are wrong for this ridge.
  • Wind shell, insulation layer, spare gloves. The summit is 1,000 metres above sea level and windy.
  • Helmet. Not ironic. Loose rock can be kicked down from above on the popular section.
  • Map, compass, and GPS. Summit ridge in cloud is not a phone-only problem.
  • Water (2 litres). No reliable source above 400 metres.
  • First aid kit. A sprained ankle 3 kilometres out and 900 metres up is a long rescue.

Fjellvettreglene on this route

Four rules of the Norwegian Mountain Code are directly load-bearing on Rørnestinden:

  • Rule 2: Adapt the trip to ability and conditions. DNT Red is the grade. Fitness and scrambling experience are baseline.
  • Rule 3: Pay attention to weather and avalanche forecasts. In ski season, this route is avalanche terrain. Check varsom.no the night before and the morning of.
  • Rule 7: Use a map and compass. Phone-only navigation is not enough on the summit ridge in cloud.
  • Rule 8: Turn back in time. The ridge is where the turnarounds happen. Do it.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Summit elevation992 m
Distance8 km round trip
Elevation gain900 m
Time5 to 6 hours round trip
DNT gradeRed (strenuous, exposed ridge)
SeasonJuly to September (summer). Ski touring February to May.
TrailheadE6 at the Kjosen valley, between Oteren and Skibotn
ParkingGravel lay-by, 8 to 10 cars, free
Trailhead GPS69.4390°N, 20.1770°E
Mobile coveragePatchy above 600 metres
Emergency112 (police/rescue), 113 (medical)

Who should hike this

  • Experienced ridge walkers. If you have done Hurrungane, Romsdalseggen, or similar, this is within your range.
  • Ski tourers in spring looking for a harder objective. The south and east aspects give a long ski descent in the right conditions.
  • Photographers wanting the view back at the glaciated peaks. Rørnestinden looks straight at Store Kjostind, Tafeltinden, and the Jiehkkevárri massif.

Who should skip this

  • Anyone without scrambling experience. The summit ridge is not optional. There is no bypass.
  • Travellers in poor weather. This is not a route to force. Wait a day.
  • First-time visitors to Lyngen in summer. Do Store Kjostind's southwest flank first. Build up.

rornestinden-lyngen-ridge-hike.metaDescription: Trail guide to Rørnestinden (992m) above the Kjosen valley in the Lyngen Alps. 8 km DNT Red with exposed ridge, 900m gain, 5 to 6 hours. Trailhead, gear, and turn-back rules.

goalsevarri-store-kjostind-ski-tour.title: Goalsevárri and Store Kjostind: the intermediate Lyngen ski tour
goalsevarri-store-kjostind-ski-tour.subtitle: The bread-and-butter ski-touring objective above Lyngseidet. 833 metres of gain, non-glaciated, south-facing corn in April. This is where Lyngen ski weeks start.
goalsevarri-store-kjostind-ski-tour.excerpt: Goalsevárri (833m) is the approach peak for Store Kjostind's southwest flank, the most-toured intermediate objective in the Lyngen Alps. 6 km, 833 m of gain, 4 to 5 hours. Non-glaciated, which means no rope team, but this is avalanche terrain. Transceiver, shovel, probe, and Level 2 skills are baseline.
goalsevarri-store-kjostind-ski-tour.content:
Goalsevárri is the first peak most ski-touring clients do when they arrive in Lyngseidet for a week-long course. 833 metres, non-glaciated, south-facing, and with a short ridge to Store Kjostind's southwest flank that gives a genuine alpine descent without requiring a rope team. From late March to late April it is one of the reliable objectives when Jiehkkevárri is out of condition.

This is not a beginner ski tour. It is the entry-level Lyngen objective, which is a different statement. Lyngen entry-level means transceiver, shovel, probe, Avalanche Level 2 skills, and enough kick-turn technique to handle 35-degree slopes under load.

The honest grade

This is avalanche terrain. The approach slope above the Kjoselva has sections in the 30 to 35 degree range with open aspect. The upper southwest flank of Store Kjostind touches 40 degrees in places. In a Norwegian Level 3 forecast (Considerable) this route is often closed by local guides. Level 2 (Moderate) is the working condition.

In summer, the route becomes a DNT Blue to Red scramble without the ski descent. The same approach slope is scree and grass, and the southwest flank becomes a short rock scramble. Most Lyngen visitors do this route in ski-touring season. The summer version is a shoulder-season consolation, not the reason to come.

The route (ski touring)

Trailhead. Park at the ski-touring lot at Lyngseidet, signed for Goalsevárri. 20 to 30 cars, free. In high season (March and April) arrive before 08:30.

Lower approach. Skin up through open birch forest along the Kjoselva for 200 metres of gain. The trail widens above the trees. 45 to 60 minutes.

Middle approach. Open slope climbing in switchbacks toward the Goalsevárri ridge. Aspect begins to matter here. South-facing in the morning, the snow will be firm. Later in the day it softens. 60 to 90 minutes.

Summit push. From the Goalsevárri sub-summit, a short ridge leads to the Store Kjostind southwest flank. This is the steeper section. 40 minutes to the summit ski-off point.

Descent. The southwest flank gives 400 to 500 metres of open-slope skiing in good conditions. Return to the approach line for the lower descent. 30 to 45 minutes to the car in good snow.

The turn-back rule

  • Varsom Level 3 or higher. The approach slope and southwest flank are both in avalanche terrain. Level 3 means rethink the objective.
  • Wind loading above 1,000 metres. Fresh snow on top of a wind slab changes the southwest flank rapidly. If the ridge is visibly drifting, turn back.
  • Whiteout on the Goalsevárri sub-summit. The terrain ahead has a convex roll that is a committing feature in poor visibility. Wait it out or reverse.
  • Soft corn above 1,000 metres before 10:00. If the overnight refreeze did not happen, the whole aspect is in reactive condition. Descend before the sun does more work.

Gear for this route

  • Transceiver, shovel, probe. Not optional. Check transceivers at the trailhead.
  • Ski-touring skis, skins, ski crampons. The crampons come out on the southwest flank in firm conditions.
  • Helmet. Worn from the summit ridge onward.
  • Avalanche airbag if you own one. Lyngen is the place it earns its weight.
  • Wind shell, insulation layer, spare gloves, goggles. Arctic March can be −15°C with wind.
  • Map of the Lyngen Alps (Turkart Lyngshalvøya, 1:50,000). Printed. In a ski-jacket pocket.

Fjellvettreglene on this route

Four rules of the Norwegian Mountain Code apply directly:

  • Rule 2: Adapt the trip to ability and conditions. The Lyngen ski objectives assume Avalanche Level 2 skills as baseline, not as ambition.
  • Rule 3: Pay attention to weather and avalanche forecasts. varsom.no publishes the regional forecast each morning. Read it.
  • Rule 4: Be equipped to help yourself and others. Transceiver, shovel, probe. Companion rescue drill before the season opens.
  • Rule 8: Turn back in time. Lyngen has fatal accidents almost every season. Most were on routes that locals would have skipped on that day's forecast.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Summit elevationGoalsevárri 833 m, Store Kjostind 1,488 m (southwest shoulder)
Distance6 km round trip
Elevation gain833 m to Goalsevárri, 1,000 m if continuing to the Store Kjostind shoulder
Time4 to 5 hours round trip
GradeSki touring: intermediate, avalanche terrain. Summer: DNT Blue approach.
SeasonLate March to late April for ski touring. Summer hiking July to September.
TrailheadLyngseidet ski-touring lot, signed from Rv868
Parking20 to 30 cars, free. Arrive before 08:30 in March and April.
Avalanche forecastvarsom.no, region: Lyngen
Mobile coverageReliable in the valley, patchy above 700 metres
Emergency112 (police/rescue), 113 (medical)

Who should ski this

  • Ski tourers with Avalanche Level 2 and transceiver practice. This is the right first Lyngen objective.
  • Clients of a certified guide. Magic Mountain Lodge, Lyngen Lodge, and international operators run Goalsevárri as a day-one objective.
  • Returning visitors in a benign forecast. If you have done this once guided, a Level 1 or 2 day with a partner is a reasonable self-led objective.

Who should skip this

  • First-time ski tourers. Lyngen is not where you learn. Take a course in Hemsedal or the Alps first.
  • Solo skiers. The approach and descent are avalanche terrain. Not a solo route.
  • Any Level 3 or 4 forecast day. Wait for the next weather window. There will be one.

Marte Åsheim is the Mountain Safety Editor at NorgeTravel. DNT-certified guide with 18 years of experience across Jotunheimen, Rondane, and the Nordic peaks. She writes trail guides so that you come home afterward. She can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

goalsevarri-store-kjostind-ski-tour.metaTitle: Goalsevárri & Store Kjostind Ski Tour 2026: Intermediate Lyngen | NorgeTravel
goalsevarri-store-kjostind-ski-tour.metaDescription: Ski-touring guide to Goalsevárri and Store Kjostind's southwest flank above Lyngseidet. 6 km, 833m gain, 4 to 5 hours. Avalanche terrain. Gear, forecast, and turn-back rules.

fastdalstinden-lyngen-ski-tour.title: Fastdalstinden: the long day above Koppangen, Lyngen Alps
fastdalstinden-lyngen-ski-tour.subtitle: A 1,100-metre non-glaciated objective on the east shore of the Lyngenfjord. Sustained climb, exposed summit ridge, and one of the longer ski descents in the range.
fastdalstinden-lyngen-ski-tour.excerpt: Fastdalstinden is 1,275 metres above Koppangen on the east side of the Lyngenfjord. 9 km round trip, 1,100 m of gain, 6 to 7 hours. DNT Red in summer, avalanche terrain in ski season. A full day for experienced tourers or fit summer scramblers.
fastdalstinden-lyngen-ski-tour.content:
Fastdalstinden is the peak that people book a second Lyngen week to do. It sits on the east side of the Lyngenfjord above Koppangen, which means it looks across the water at the Lyngseidet peninsula and the bigger glaciated faces. 1,275 metres, non-glaciated, with a sustained 1,100-metre climb from the trailhead and a long ski descent in the right conditions. This is a full day.

If Goalsevárri is the Lyngen entry-level objective, Fastdalstinden is the intermediate-plus test. Longer, steeper at the top, and with a committing summit ridge. Skiers and scramblers both come here for the same reason: the fjord view from the summit is one of the best in Troms.

The honest grade

DNT Red in summer: strenuous, sustained, with a scramble on the upper ridge. In ski season, this is avalanche terrain from roughly 600 metres upward, with the final 200 metres of the summit push on aspects that can hold wind slab into May. Fitness matters. A 1,100-metre ski-tour day with a long descent is not a half-day objective.

The upper ridge in summer has short scrambling sections without fixed protection. The drop on the east side is sustained enough that a slip would not be recoverable. Steady movement, hands on the rock.

The route (ski touring)

Trailhead. Park at Koppangen harbour on the east side of the Lyngenfjord, reached by the ferry from Svensby or by the road around via Skibotn. Small lot, 10 to 12 cars.

Lower approach. Skin up through birch for 300 metres of gain, then onto open terrain above the tree line. 60 to 80 minutes.

Middle approach. Long open-slope switchbacks up the Fastdalen valley. Gradient increases. 90 to 120 minutes.

Upper flank. The summit push is on a steeper aspect, 35 to 40 degrees in sections. Ski crampons useful in firm conditions. 45 minutes.

Summit ridge. Short ski or boot to the summit cairn depending on snow. Exposed to both sides. 15 minutes.

Descent. On a good corn day the descent gives 800 to 900 metres of near-continuous skiing back toward Koppangen. 45 minutes to the car in good snow.

The route (summer)

The same line as the ski approach is walked on summer ground. Birch, then tundra, then scree, then rock scramble on the upper ridge. No marked trail above the tree line. Navigation is by terrain reading and cairns. 6 to 7 hours for the full round trip.

The turn-back rule

  • Varsom Level 3 or higher. The upper flank is in avalanche terrain. Level 3 means rethink.
  • Wind slab on the summit aspect. Visible drifting on the upper ridge is a turn-back signal. The windward aspect clears, the lee aspect loads.
  • Whiteout in the Fastdalen valley. The approach is navigable in poor visibility, but the summit ridge is not. Turn back at the sub-summit if you cannot see it.
  • Late start. If you are not on the trail by 09:00 in April, the south aspects will be in wet-snow condition by early afternoon. Reverse before they cycle.

Gear for this route

  • Transceiver, shovel, probe. Non-negotiable for the ski-season route.
  • Ski crampons. The upper flank is firm in the mornings.
  • Helmet. Worn from the mid-approach onward.
  • Airbag pack if you own one.
  • Wind shell, insulation layer, spare gloves, goggles.
  • Headlamp. A 7-hour day with a late start can finish in fading light in March.
  • 2 to 3 litres of water, snacks for a full day.

Fjellvettreglene on this route

  • Rule 1: Plan and communicate your route. Tell someone at Lyngseidet or your accommodation your plan for the day.
  • Rule 3: Pay attention to weather and avalanche forecasts. varsom.no region Lyngen. Check twice: night before and morning of.
  • Rule 4: Be equipped to help yourself and others. Companion rescue drill, first aid kit, repair kit.
  • Rule 8: Turn back in time. Fastdalstinden is a long day. The last hour of climbing is when fatigue meets exposure. If either is wrong, turn back.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Summit elevation1,275 m
Distance9 km round trip
Elevation gain1,100 m
Time6 to 7 hours round trip
GradeSki touring: intermediate-plus, avalanche terrain. Summer: DNT Red with upper-ridge scramble.
SeasonLate March to early May for ski touring. Summer hiking July to September.
TrailheadKoppangen harbour, east shore of the Lyngenfjord
AccessSvensby–Breivikeidet ferry from Tromsø, then road to Lyngseidet and around the fjord. 2 hours from Tromsø.
ParkingKoppangen harbour, 10 to 12 cars, free
Avalanche forecastvarsom.no, region: Lyngen
Mobile coverageGood at the trailhead, patchy above 700 metres
Emergency112 (police/rescue), 113 (medical)

Who should do this route

  • Returning Lyngen ski tourers. After Goalsevárri and a couple of warm-up days, this is the next step.
  • Fit summer scramblers. If you like Romsdalseggen or Store Skagastølstind's Fannaråken approach, you will like Fastdalstinden.
  • Guided groups. This is a day-three or day-four objective on a well-run ski week.

Who should skip this

  • Anyone on day one of a Lyngen trip. The fitness demand is real. Start elsewhere.
  • Ski tourers without ski-crampon experience. The upper flank in firm conditions is where crampons earn their weight. Practice in less committing terrain first.
  • Any forecast day above Varsom Level 3. Not this route. Not this day.

Marte Åsheim is the Mountain Safety Editor at NorgeTravel. DNT-certified guide with 18 years of experience across Jotunheimen, Rondane, and the Nordic peaks. She writes trail guides so that you come home afterward. She can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

fastdalstinden-lyngen-ski-tour.metaTitle: Fastdalstinden Ski Tour 2026: 1,275m Above Koppangen, Lyngen | NorgeTravel
fastdalstinden-lyngen-ski-tour.metaDescription: Trail guide to Fastdalstinden (1,275m) above Koppangen on the east Lyngenfjord shore. 9 km, 1,100m gain, 6 to 7 hours. Ski touring or summer scramble. Gear and turn-back rules.

sorlenangsbreen-glacier-lyngen.title: Sørlenangsbreen: the guided glacier approach on the Lyngen peninsula
sorlenangsbreen-glacier-lyngen.subtitle: A glaciated objective on the north end of the Lyngen Alps. Rope team, harness, crevasse rescue. Guide mandatory unless you have alpine experience. This is the real Lyngen.
sorlenangsbreen-glacier-lyngen.excerpt: Sørlenangsbreen is the glaciated north end of the Lyngen range, accessed from the Lenangen side of the peninsula. 12 km round trip, 950 m of gain, 7 to 8 hours, crevassed glacier terrain. Not optional: rope team, harness, and crevasse rescue skills. Book a certified guide.
sorlenangsbreen-glacier-lyngen.content:
Glacier objective. Guide mandatory.

Sørlenangsbreen is a crevassed glacier. A solo crossing without rope-team experience and crevasse rescue practice is not a hike. It is a fatal accident waiting for conditions to align. Book through a certified Lyngen operator. Fjellvettreglene Rule 5 is the rule of law here.

Sørlenangsbreen sits at the north end of the Lyngen range, a crevassed glacier that drops from around 1,400 metres to 900 metres on the east aspect. It is one of several glacier objectives that separate Lyngen from its neighbouring ranges. Most Lyngen visitors never set foot on a glacier, because the non-glaciated peaks around Lyngseidet are enough. The travellers who do come for Sørlenangsbreen are either booked with a certified guide or have genuine alpine experience they are transferring in.

This guide is written to help you make the right decision about whether this objective belongs in your Lyngen week, and to brief the conversation you will have with a local guide if you decide it does.

The honest grade

This is not a DNT-graded hike. DNT does not grade glaciated terrain beyond Sort (black, expert alpine). Sørlenangsbreen is a Level 2 to Level 3 glacier crossing depending on the season and the route chosen. It is the bread-and-butter of guided Lyngen alpine weeks, not a self-led objective for visitors without a roped-team background.

In a normal spring, the glacier is covered in snow that bridges the crevasses. This is the window when most guided ascents happen: late March through May. By midsummer the bridges weaken and the route becomes technically easier but navigation-wise harder. In autumn the glacier is dry ice with exposed crevasses. Few parties cross it at that stage.

Why a guide is mandatory

Four specific risks make this route a guided objective:

  • Crevasses. Sørlenangsbreen has known crevasse fields on the lower and middle sections. Hidden crevasses under snow bridges are the classic glacier fatality. A solo traveller who falls into a crevasse will die there.
  • Rope-team movement. A two or three-person rope team with proper spacing, coordinated pacing, and the ability to execute a Z-pulley crevasse rescue under load is a skill set that takes multiple training days to acquire. It cannot be learned from a video.
  • Route finding. The safe line across a glacier shifts season to season. Local guides know which fields close by May and which bridges to avoid in July.
  • Rescue response time. Lyngen is remote. The Norwegian Red Cross rescue helicopter based in Tromsø is the nearest response, and weather grounds it frequently. A guided team's ability to self-rescue is the difference between a bad day and a fatality.

Who runs guided ascents

  • Magic Mountain Lodge (Lyngseidet). Runs guided glacier days as part of week-long ski-touring programmes. Certified IFMGA guides on staff.
  • Lyngen Lodge (Djupvik). Similar guided programme, boat access from the west side of the fjord.
  • Mountain Tracks, 57hours, Elemental Adventure. International operators who run dedicated Lyngen alpine weeks with IFMGA or UIAGM guides.
  • Local IFMGA-certified guides. Bookable directly for day trips if you have the prior experience to make that case.

Expect a day-rate of 4,500 to 6,500 NOK per person for a small group, or 12,000 to 16,000 NOK for a private guide with one or two clients. This is not a cheap objective. It is a correct objective.

What a guided day looks like

Morning brief. Guide reviews the varsom.no forecast, the meteorological outlook, and the chosen line. Equipment check: harness, rope, crampons, ice axe, transceiver (in ski season), helmet.

Approach (2 to 3 hours). From the Lenangen trailhead on the north end of the Lyngen peninsula, the approach climbs through birch and onto the moraine below the glacier tongue. Approximately 500 metres of gain before the ice.

Rope-up (30 minutes). At the glacier edge, the team ties in, discusses pacing and spacing, and reviews the signal set for a suspected crevasse fall.

Glacier crossing (2 to 3 hours). Roped travel across Sørlenangsbreen on the chosen line. Pacing is set by the guide.

Descent (2 hours). Reverse the approach or, on a ski-touring day, ski the glacier and the lower flanks back to the trailhead.

The turn-back rule (guided)

On a guided day, the guide owns the turn-back decision. You should also know what the triggers are:

  • Temperature rise above 0°C at glacier elevation before midday. Snow bridges weaken rapidly.
  • Fresh snowfall of 20 cm or more in the previous 24 hours. New snow hides old crevasses and loads lee slopes.
  • Visibility below 200 metres on the glacier. Route finding becomes guesswork.
  • Wind above 15 m/s at glacier level. Standing rope-team communication becomes difficult.

Fjellvettreglene on this route

  • Rule 1: Plan and communicate your route. The guide files the plan. You confirm it.
  • Rule 5: Travel rope-linked in glacial terrain. This is the rule of law here. Non-negotiable.
  • Rule 3: Pay attention to weather and avalanche forecasts. The guide checks varsom.no, yr.no, and met.no that morning.
  • Rule 8: Turn back in time. On a glacier, turn-back is not a failure. It is the correct reading of conditions.

The logistics

DetailInformation
Glacier high pointApproximately 1,400 m on the standard guided line
Distance12 km round trip
Elevation gain950 m
Time7 to 8 hours, guided
GradeGlaciated alpine. Rope team mandatory. Guide mandatory for non-alpinists.
SeasonLate March through early July is the stable snow-bridge window.
TrailheadLenangen, north end of the Lyngen peninsula, Rv91 / Rv868
AccessVia Lyngseidet by road, 45 minutes north
Guide day rate4,500 to 6,500 NOK per person in a small group. 12,000 to 16,000 NOK private.
Avalanche forecastvarsom.no, region: Lyngen
RescueNorwegian Red Cross, helicopter Tromsø. 112 (police/rescue), 113 (medical).

Who should do this route

  • Clients on a guided Lyngen alpine week. This is one of the signature days.
  • Alpine skiers or mountaineers with prior glacier-travel training. Even with experience, a day with a local guide for route knowledge is a good call.
  • Photographers travelling with an IFMGA guide for access. The glacier terrain gives a specific landscape that the fjord-side peaks do not.

Who should skip this

  • Anyone without roped-team training. Book the Goalsevárri or Rørnestinden objective instead. You will not miss the essence of Lyngen by skipping the glacier.
  • Solo travellers. Solo glacier travel in Lyngen is a line that local guides refuse to cross, and for correct reasons.
  • Visitors on a tight budget. The guide fee is the right cost for the risk. If the cost is wrong for your trip, the objective is wrong for your trip.

Marte Åsheim is the Mountain Safety Editor at NorgeTravel. DNT-certified guide with 18 years of experience across Jotunheimen, Rondane, and the Nordic peaks. She writes trail guides so that you come home afterward. She can be reached at hei@norgetravel.com.

segla-senja-ridge-hike.title: Segla: the ridge hike above Fjordgård, Senja
segla-senja-ridge-hike.subtitle: The signature Senja peak. 639 metres, 6 km round trip, a narrow final ridge straight above the Mefjord. DNT Blue with a scramble finish.
segla-senja-ridge-hike.excerpt: Segla rises 639 metres straight out of the Mefjord above Fjordgård. 6 km round trip, 4 to 5 hours, DNT Blue with an exposed scramble to the cairn. The trailhead car park fills by 08:00 in July. Come early or come in September.
segla-senja-ridge-hike.content:
Segla is the peak every photograph of Senja is trying to capture. 639 metres rising out of the Mefjord, a blade of rock with a sharp profile from the west and a narrow summit ridge that makes the last 15 minutes feel alpine even though the grade on paper is DNT Blue.

The numbers are modest. The consequences of the summit scramble are not. This guide gives the route, the turn-back trigger, and what the Fjordgård trailhead looks like at 06:00 versus 10:00 in July.

The honest grade

Segla is DNT Blue on the approach. The final 100 metres is a scramble on rock with drop-offs on both sides. No fixed chains. Dry rock is straightforward for anyone comfortable with scrambling. Wet rock changes the calculation. In rain, the summit block becomes a Red-grade problem and people turn back.

If you are not used to exposure, do Hesten on the opposite side of the Mefjord first. Hesten gives you the classic Segla photograph without the scramble.

The route

Trailhead. Fjordgård village, at the head of the Mefjord on the north coast of Senja. Follow signs for the Segla parking. The car park holds roughly 80 cars and fills between 08:00 and 18:00 from late June through mid-August.

Lower section. Marked path through birch forest and open meadow. 250 metres of gain in the first 45 minutes. Boardwalks across the boggy sections.

Middle section. Above the treeline the path climbs across open slope to the base of the summit ridge. Red T-marks and cairns. 60 to 75 minutes.

Summit ridge. The last 100 metres is the hands-on section. Narrow, with exposure on both sides. Drop to the Mefjord on one side, drop to the village on the other. 15 to 20 minutes to the cairn.

Descent. Same route. Most people move slower going down the scramble. Budget 2 to 2.5 hours back.

The turn-back rule

Rain on the summit block. Wet rock is the trigger. If it starts raining during the ridge section, turn around immediately. The photograph is not worth the slip. This is Fjellvettreglene Rule 8: turn back in time, no shame in retreat.

Gear

  • Proper hiking boots, not trainers. The final section has loose rock.
  • Windproof layer. The ridge catches weather moving in off the Atlantic.
  • Water. 1.5 litres minimum in summer.
  • Map: Turkart Senja 1:50,000. Do not rely on phone only.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 1: Plan. Check yr.no the night before and morning of. Rule 3: Read the weather. A forecast of showers between 12:00 and 14:00 means start at 06:00 or postpone. Rule 8: Turn back on wet rock.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadFjordgård, signed Segla parking
Parking~80 cars, fee, fills by 08:00 in peak summer
SeasonLate June to mid-October. Snow lingers in gullies until early July.
Nearest fuelBotnhamn or Gryllefjord
Emergency112. Nearest hospital: Tromsø (UNN)

Who should do Segla

Intermediate hikers comfortable with short scrambles. Anyone who does not mind a crowded trailhead. Photographers who want the ridge shot from the top rather than from across the fjord.

Who should skip it

Anyone uncomfortable on exposed rock. Families with young children — do Hesten instead. Anyone arriving on a rainy forecast: postpone rather than force it.
segla-senja-ridge-hike.featuredImageAlt: Hikers on the narrow summit ridge of Segla above the Mefjord, Senja
segla-senja-ridge-hike.metaDescription: Segla hike guide, Senja: 639m peak above Fjordgård. 6 km, 600m gain, 4 to 5 hours, DNT Blue with scramble. Parking, turn-back rule, Fjellvettreglene.

husfjellet-senja-hike.title: Husfjellet: the plateau peak above Skaland, Senja
husfjellet-senja-hike.subtitle: A DNT Blue hike to a 635-metre plateau summit above Skaland with open views across to Okshornan. No scrambling. Boardwalks on the lower section.
husfjellet-senja-hike.excerpt: Husfjellet is the family-friendly Senja peak. 635 metres, 5 km round trip, 3 to 4 hours, DNT Blue on a good path with boardwalks across the bogs. Open plateau summit looking directly at the Okshornan ridge. No scrambling.
husfjellet-senja-hike.content:
Husfjellet is the Senja hike to do when Segla is rained off or the Fjordgård car park is full. It is DNT Blue, it is boardwalked across the boggy lower section, and the summit is a broad plateau rather than a ridge scramble. The view from the top looks straight across the Bergsfjord at the Okshornan range, which is the other silhouette everyone photographs on Senja.

This is the right peak for a mixed-ability group and for a cloudy day with a forecast that does not justify Segla.

The honest grade

DNT Blue. No scrambling. No exposure. Steady climb on a marked path for the first two-thirds, then open plateau for the last push. The boardwalks mean the bog at 200 to 350 metres does not ruin your boots.

This does not mean the weather is friendly. The plateau is fully exposed. If the wind is 15 m/s at sea level it will be 25 on top. The summit does not offer shelter.

The route

Trailhead. Skaland, on the northwest coast of Senja. Park at the signed car park off the old mine road above the village. Roughly 30 cars, fee in summer.

Lower section. Birch forest and open slope on a marked T-path. Boardwalks across the wet section between 200 and 350 metres. 45 to 60 minutes.

Upper section. Open tundra climbing to the plateau edge. Cairns mark the route. 45 minutes to the plateau rim.

Summit plateau. The true summit is a 10-minute walk across the plateau from where the path crests the rim. Do walk to the actual cairn — the Okshornan view is better from there.

Descent. Same route. 1.5 to 2 hours.

Gear

  • Waterproof boots. The boardwalks help but not all of it is boarded.
  • Windproof layer, always.
  • Map: Turkart Senja 1:50,000.
  • Water. 1 to 1.5 litres.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 2: adapt the trip to the conditions. Husfjellet is often the correct choice when Segla is forecast wet. Rule 3: read the weather — wind on the plateau is the real variable.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadSkaland, old mine road car park
Parking~30 cars, fee
SeasonMid-June to mid-October
Nearest fuelSilsand or Gryllefjord
Emergency112. Nearest A&E: Tromsø (UNN)

Who should do Husfjellet

Families with teenagers, mixed-ability groups, anyone who wants a real Senja peak without the Segla scramble, and photographers who want the Okshornan shot from across the fjord.

Who should skip it

Anyone already comfortable with exposure who specifically came for Segla. Do Segla first, then Husfjellet as a rest-day option.
husfjellet-senja-hike.featuredImageAlt: Hikers on the open plateau summit of Husfjellet above Skaland with the Okshornan ridge across the water
husfjellet-senja-hike.metaDescription: Husfjellet hike guide, Senja: 635m plateau peak above Skaland. 5 km, 600m gain, 3 to 4 hours, DNT Blue. Trailhead, boardwalks, views to Okshornan.

hesten-senja-viewpoint-hike.title: Hesten: the viewpoint peak opposite Segla, Senja
hesten-senja-viewpoint-hike.subtitle: 556 metres on the north side of the Mefjord. The best photograph of Segla is from here. DNT Blue, 7 km round trip, 4 hours.
hesten-senja-viewpoint-hike.excerpt: Hesten is 556 metres on the opposite side of the Mefjord from Segla. 7 km round trip, 4 hours, DNT Blue. The summit gives the classic Segla photograph because Segla fills the frame from your own summit cairn.
hesten-senja-viewpoint-hike.content:
If your reason for being on Senja is to photograph Segla, you want to be on Hesten. From the Segla summit itself, Segla fills the frame you are standing on. From Hesten, across the Mefjord, Segla rises as the sharp blade of rock that built Senja's reputation. 556 metres, DNT Blue, 4 hours round trip on a good path.

This is the right hike if you are on a family trip, arrived too late to get a Segla car-park spot, or want the photograph rather than the summit.

The honest grade

DNT Blue. Marked T-path throughout. One short steep section in the middle third. No scrambling, no exposure on the summit. The summit is broad enough for a group to spread out and set up tripods.

The route

Trailhead. Fjordgård, the same village as Segla, but a different car park on the north side of the village. Signed for Hesten. Smaller car park than Segla, roughly 40 cars, fills later because the hike is less Instagrammed.

Lower section. Marked path climbing through birch forest and open slope. 300 metres of gain in 60 minutes.

Middle section. Steeper open slope with cairns. 200 metres of gain in 40 minutes.

Summit. Broad flat summit with a clear view across to Segla, the Mefjord below, and the open sea to the north. Allow 30 to 60 minutes on top if the light is right.

Descent. Same route. 1.5 hours.

Timing for photographs

The Segla face is east-facing from Hesten. In midsummer, morning light on Segla is 05:00 to 09:00. The midnight sun side-lights the ridge from the north between 22:00 and 01:00. Cloud on the Segla summit is a feature, not a problem — Segla in cloud with clear light on the ridge is the better photograph.

Gear

  • Hiking boots.
  • Windproof layer.
  • Tripod if you are shooting. The summit is broad.
  • Map: Turkart Senja 1:50,000.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 1: plan and communicate your route. Rule 4: be equipped — even on a 4-hour hike, a windproof and a head torch in shoulder season are not optional.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadFjordgård, Hesten car park (north side of village)
Parking~40 cars, fee
SeasonMid-June to mid-October
Nearest fuelBotnhamn or Gryllefjord
Emergency112. Nearest A&E: Tromsø (UNN)

Who should do Hesten

Photographers targeting Segla. Families who want a real peak without the scramble. Anyone arriving at Fjordgård after 09:00 in July, when the Segla car park is already full.

Who should skip it

If you have only one day and you are a strong hiker comfortable with exposure, do Segla. Come back another day for Hesten.
hesten-senja-viewpoint-hike.featuredImageAlt: View across the Mefjord from Hesten with Segla rising on the far side
hesten-senja-viewpoint-hike.metaDescription: Hesten hike guide, Senja: 556m viewpoint peak across the Mefjord from Segla. 7 km, 550m gain, 4 hours, DNT Blue. The best Segla photograph is from Hesten.

barden-senja-ridge-hike.title: Barden: the long ridge day on northern Senja
barden-senja-ridge-hike.subtitle: 775 metres above Ersfjord. A DNT Red day with a long approach, exposed final ridge, and full Fjellvettreglene protocol. Summer dry conditions only.
barden-senja-ridge-hike.excerpt: Barden is the serious hike on northern Senja. 775 metres, 11 km round trip, 5 to 6 hours, DNT Red. Long approach, steep final ridge, real exposure. Summer dry conditions only. Full Fjellvettreglene protocol applies.
barden-senja-ridge-hike.content:
Barden is the hike on northern Senja that most guidebooks leave off. 775 metres above Ersfjord, a long approach across open ground, a steep final ridge with real exposure, and a summit that looks down on the Okshornan spires from the inland side. DNT Red. This is the big day out for anyone who came to Senja and already did Segla.

It is also the hike where the weather decides the day. Rain turns the approach slope into wet grass and the upper ridge into a serious proposition. The turn-back decision on Barden is real.

The honest grade

DNT Red. Strenuous, with scrambling and exposure on the final 150 metres. Long approach. The path is marked but in places the line becomes indistinct across open slope. In cloud, navigation on the upper section is a real problem. This is a hike for competent map-and-compass users, not phone-GPS tourists.

The route

Trailhead. Ersfjord, on the north coast of Senja. Park near the bridge at the head of Ersfjordstranda. Limited spaces, arrive early.

Lower section. The path climbs steadily across open grass and birch scrub for the first 2 km. 300 metres of gain in 75 minutes.

Middle section. Long traverse across open slope and heather, slowly gaining height. 250 metres of gain across 3 km. This is where the path becomes faint. Cairns mark the key turns.

Upper ridge. Final 150 metres is steep with hands-on sections. Exposure on the north side. 45 to 60 minutes.

Summit. Small cairn, drops on three sides. The view runs from Okshornan to the north coast and inland to the Senja plateau. 20 to 30 minutes on top is enough.

Descent. Same route. 2.5 hours. Most people misjudge the descent time and end up running the last hour. Do not.

The turn-back rule

Three triggers. One: cloud closes on the upper ridge before you reach it. The navigation problem is real and the exposure is not worth forcing. Two: rain starts on the approach. The upper ridge on wet rock is a different grade entirely. Three: any member of the group is moving noticeably slower than planned at the 2-hour mark. You will not get back by 20:00 and the late descent in wet grass is where ankles break.

Gear

  • Proper hiking boots with ankle support. Not trainers, not approach shoes.
  • Full waterproof shell plus windproof layer.
  • Map and compass. Turkart Senja 1:50,000 minimum. Phone GPS is a backup, not the primary tool.
  • Head torch in shoulder season — 15:00 in October is close to dark.
  • 2 litres of water, food for 6 hours.
  • First-aid kit with a blister plaster supply. The descent chews feet.

Fjellvettreglene applied

All nine rules apply on Barden. The load-bearing ones: Rule 1 (plan and tell someone your route), Rule 3 (check the weather the night before and on the day), Rule 4 (be equipped to help yourself), Rule 7 (map and compass, not just phone), and Rule 8 (turn back in time).

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadErsfjord bridge car park
ParkingLimited, ~15 cars. Arrive by 08:00.
SeasonJuly to mid-September, dry conditions
Nearest fuelSilsand or Gryllefjord
Emergency112. Nearest A&E: Tromsø (UNN)

Who should do Barden

Fit hikers who have already done Segla and want a bigger day. Anyone comfortable with a DNT Red ridge and competent with a paper map.

Who should skip it

First-time Senja hikers. Anyone without recent ridge-scrambling experience. Anyone who cannot commit to turning back on the trigger conditions above.
barden-senja-ridge-hike.featuredImageAlt: The Okshornan ridge on northern Senja with steep summit spires and ocean below
barden-senja-ridge-hike.metaDescription: Barden hike guide, Senja: 775m DNT Red peak above Ersfjord. 11 km, 775m gain, 5 to 6 hours, exposed ridge. Turn-back rule and Fjellvettreglene checklist.

haldde-alta-observatory-hike.title: Haldde: the aurora observatory summit above Kåfjord, Alta
haldde-alta-observatory-hike.subtitle: A DNT Blue climb to the first permanent aurora research station in the world. 904 metres, 10 km round trip, stone ruins on top, sight lines across Altafjord to the Seiland glacier.
haldde-alta-observatory-hike.excerpt: Haldde is 904 metres above Kåfjord, west of Alta. 10 km round trip, 5 to 6 hours, DNT Blue. The summit holds the stone ruins of the world's first permanent aurora observatory, built in 1899. A solid intermediate Finnmark hike with a genuine historical payoff.
haldde-alta-observatory-hike.content:
Haldde is 904 metres on the west side of Altafjord, above Kåfjord. The summit ruin is the reason to climb it. In 1899, Norwegian physicist Kristian Birkeland built the world's first permanent aurora observatory on this peak. The stone walls and foundation are still there, weathered but standing. You walk through the doorway Birkeland walked through to collect magnetic and auroral readings that fed his theory of charged solar particles — the foundation of modern aurora science.

The hike itself is DNT Blue, long but not technical. This is the Alta hike for travellers who want a real Finnmark peak day without glacier gear or alpine exposure.

The honest grade

DNT Blue. Marked path, steady climb, one steeper section on the upper third, no scrambling, no exposure. Long day: 900 metres of vertical gain is real work. The upper plateau is fully exposed to weather off the Barents Sea. Wind on the summit can be double what it is at the car park.

The route

Trailhead. From Alta, drive the E6 west and take the Rv882 toward Talvik. Park at the Haldde trailhead near Kåfjord village. Roughly 25 spaces, free.

Lower section. Marked T-path climbs through birch forest, crosses the treeline at 350 metres. 75 to 90 minutes.

Middle section. Open tundra on a clear path with cairns. Steady climb. 90 minutes.

Upper plateau. The final 150 metres opens onto the plateau with the observatory ruin at the high point. Walk up to the doorway — the view north across the fjord to the Seiland glacier on a clear day is the payoff.

Descent. Same route. 2 to 2.5 hours.

The history

Kristian Birkeland built two observatories on Haldde in 1899 to measure the aurora and the magnetic field simultaneously. He overwintered teams there through the polar night. His work proved the aurora was caused by charged particles from the sun deflected by the earth's magnetic field — decades before the idea was accepted internationally. The stone ruins on the summit are what is left. Respect them. Do not stack cairns on the walls.

Gear

  • Hiking boots with ankle support.
  • Full waterproof shell, windproof layer. The plateau weather changes fast.
  • 1.5 to 2 litres water.
  • Head torch in September and October — daylight runs short.
  • Turkart Alta 1:50,000.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 1: plan and tell someone. Rule 3: check the weather — the summit weather is rarely the car park weather. Rule 4: equipment for self-rescue even on a Blue. Rule 8: turn back on the plateau if cloud closes — navigation is not dangerous but visibility kills the view, and the point of Haldde is the view and the ruin.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadKåfjord, signed Haldde parking (Rv882)
Parking~25 spaces, free
SeasonLate June to early October
Drive from Alta35 minutes
Emergency112. Nearest hospital: Alta

Who should do Haldde

Anyone interested in the aurora. Anyone who wants a real Finnmark peak day without alpine gear. Fit intermediate hikers.

Who should skip it

Families with young children — do Komsafjellet instead. Anyone short on time: Haldde needs a full day plus the drive.
haldde-alta-observatory-hike.featuredImageAlt: View across the Alta region toward glaciated peaks, with the Haldde ridge country in the distance
haldde-alta-observatory-hike.metaDescription: Haldde hike guide, Alta: 904m aurora observatory summit above Kåfjord. 10 km, 900m gain, 5 to 6 hours, DNT Blue. History, route, Fjellvettreglene.

komsafjellet-alta-short-hike.title: Komsafjellet: the short hike above Alta town
komsafjellet-alta-short-hike.subtitle: A DNT Green leg-stretcher from central Alta. 212 metres, 4 km round trip, 1 to 2 hours. Fjord view and the Hjemmeluft rock art site below.
komsafjellet-alta-short-hike.excerpt: Komsafjellet is the 212-metre hill above central Alta. 4 km round trip, 1 to 2 hours, DNT Green. The perfect leg-stretcher after landing at Alta airport. View over Altafjord and straight down onto the Hjemmeluft UNESCO rock art site.
komsafjellet-alta-short-hike.content:
Komsafjellet is the small hill directly above central Alta. 212 metres, family-friendly, and close enough to the town centre that you can walk to the trailhead from most hotels. The summit looks down on Altafjord and, directly below to the west, the Hjemmeluft UNESCO rock carving site. This is the hike to do on arrival day when your flight lands at midday and you want to move before dinner.

The honest grade

DNT Green. Marked path, modest gain, open views on the upper section. Suitable for children over 6 and for travellers who landed that morning. The upper section can be icy in shoulder season — use micro-spikes in October and April.

The route

Trailhead. Several options. The most used is from Bossekop via the signed path above the cemetery. There is also a Hjemmeluft-side approach that passes the rock art visitor centre. Both converge near the summit.

Ascent. Steady climb on a marked path through birch scrub. Open ground from 150 metres up. 45 to 60 minutes to the cairn.

Summit. Broad summit with fjord views, a flagpole, and benches. 20 to 30 minutes on top.

Descent. Same route or down the Hjemmeluft side if you want to end at the rock art visitor centre. 40 minutes.

Combine with the rock art site

The Hjemmeluft UNESCO site — carvings dating back roughly 7,000 years — sits at the base of Komsafjellet on the fjord side. Budget two hours for the visitor centre and the outdoor carvings path. Doing the hike and the rock art together makes a solid half-day.

Gear

  • Walking shoes or trainers in summer. Hiking boots in shoulder season.
  • Light waterproof.
  • Water, snack.
  • Micro-spikes in October, November, March, and April.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 2: adapt the trip to conditions. Komsafjellet is the correct choice on a short, cloudy, or tired day. Rule 4: even on a Green, a waterproof is not optional in Finnmark.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadBossekop or Hjemmeluft approach, central Alta
ParkingStreet parking or Hjemmeluft visitor centre car park
SeasonYear-round. Micro-spikes in shoulder season.
Drive from Alta airport10 minutes
Emergency112. Hospital in central Alta.

Who should do Komsafjellet

Families, arrival-day travellers, anyone combining the hike with the Hjemmeluft rock art visit. Solid choice in poor weather when the higher peaks are off the table.

Who should skip it

Travellers with only one half-day in Alta who came for a real mountain — do Haldde instead.
komsafjellet-alta-short-hike.featuredImageAlt: Overview of Alta town and the fjord with Komsafjellet rising above
komsafjellet-alta-short-hike.metaDescription: Komsafjellet hike guide, Alta: 212m hill above town. 4 km, 200m gain, 1 to 2 hours, DNT Green. Family-friendly leg-stretcher with Hjemmeluft rock art views.

rafsbotnfjellet-alta-family-hike.title: Rafsbotnfjellet: the family peak east of Alta
rafsbotnfjellet-alta-family-hike.subtitle: A DNT Blue family hike 20 minutes east of Alta. 558 metres, 8 km round trip, 3 to 4 hours. Birch forest to an open plateau with cairn and fjord views.
rafsbotnfjellet-alta-family-hike.excerpt: Rafsbotnfjellet is the family-friendly Blue-grade hike 20 minutes east of Alta. 558 metres, 8 km round trip, 3 to 4 hours. Marked trail through birch forest to an open plateau summit with cairn and view down the fjord. Snow-free late May to mid-October.
rafsbotnfjellet-alta-family-hike.content:
Rafsbotnfjellet sits 20 minutes east of central Alta at the head of Rafsbotn. 558 metres, DNT Blue, marked all the way. This is the right peak for a mixed family group that wants a real summit without the commitment of Haldde. Birch forest on the way up, open plateau on top, clear view down Altafjord.

The honest grade

DNT Blue. Marked T-path, steady climb, no scrambling. The middle third is the steepest section. The summit plateau is broad and forgiving — easy to navigate in fair weather, less so in cloud.

The route

Trailhead. Park at the signed Rafsbotnfjellet trailhead off the E6, at the head of Rafsbotn east of Alta. Small car park, roughly 15 spaces, free.

Lower section. Path climbs through birch forest on a firm trail. Boardwalks across two wet sections. 250 metres of gain in the first hour.

Middle section. The trail leaves the treeline at 350 metres and climbs open slope on a cairned line. 45 minutes.

Summit. Broad plateau summit with a cairn and an informal summit book in a metal tube. Views down the fjord and across to the Haldde side.

Descent. Same route. 1.5 hours.

Gear

  • Hiking boots.
  • Waterproof, windproof.
  • 1.5 litres water.
  • Snacks — boreal kids are usually hungry on the plateau.
  • Turkart Alta 1:50,000.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 2: adapt the trip to the group. Rafsbotnfjellet is the correct Blue when Haldde is too long for the slowest walker. Rule 3: the plateau catches weather off the plateau interior — read yr.no on the morning of.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadRafsbotn, signed from E6
Parking~15 spaces, free
SeasonLate May to mid-October
Drive from Alta20 minutes
Emergency112. Alta hospital.

Who should do Rafsbotnfjellet

Families with older kids. Travellers who want a real DNT Blue summit day without a full 10 km hike. Anyone combining a morning hike with a Sorrisniva river-boat afternoon.

Who should skip it

Experienced hikers who specifically came to Finnmark for the bigger days — do Haldde or Storvikaksla instead.
rafsbotnfjellet-alta-family-hike.featuredImageAlt: Open plateau and fjord view east of Alta in Finnmark
rafsbotnfjellet-alta-family-hike.metaDescription: Rafsbotnfjellet hike guide, Alta: 558m family peak 20 min east of town. 8 km, 550m gain, 3 to 4 hours, DNT Blue. Trailhead, season, Fjellvettreglene.

storvikaksla-seiland-ridge-hike.title: Storvikaksla: the Seiland island ridge day, west of Alta
storvikaksla-seiland-ridge-hike.subtitle: 750 metres on Seiland island. A DNT Red day requiring the ferry, solid navigation, and a weather window. Empty terrain, Arctic fox country, glaciated summits in the distance.
storvikaksla-seiland-ridge-hike.excerpt: Storvikaksla is 750 metres on Seiland island west of Alta. 14 km round trip, 6 to 7 hours, DNT Red. Requires the ferry from Hakkstabben. Empty Finnmark terrain, Arctic fox country, and the glaciated Seilandsjøkelen icecap in sight. Solid navigation and a weather window mandatory.
storvikaksla-seiland-ridge-hike.content:
Seiland is the glaciated island west of Alta. Most travellers to Alta never set foot on it. The ferry from Hakkstabben runs a handful of times a day to Eidvågen, and the island has roughly 50 permanent residents, one road, and Seilandsjøkelen — the northernmost icecap in mainland Norway. Storvikaksla is a 750-metre ridge summit that gives you the empty Finnmark day without the glacier gear requirement.

This is DNT Red. Navigation matters. The weather window matters more. Plan the ferries properly or you will be stuck on the island overnight.

The honest grade

DNT Red. Long day, limited trail marking on the upper sections, real route-finding across open ground. No scrambling of note, but the cumulative demands — distance, navigation, remoteness — put this above the Blue grade firmly. Weather moves fast off the Barents Sea and the island has no shelter on the upper plateau.

The route

Trailhead. Ferry from Hakkstabben (Stjernøya side) or Bekkarfjord (mainland) to Eidvågen or Hønseby on Seiland. Confirm the crossing the day before on the Boreal or Torghatten Nord schedule — sailings are limited and cancel in high wind.

Lower section. From the landing, walk the island road west, then pick up the marked trail toward Storvika. 4 km of road and trail across rolling ground. 90 minutes.

Middle section. Open tundra climbing gradually toward the ridge base. Cairned line, indistinct in places. Compass work mandatory in cloud. 2 hours.

Summit ridge. Final 200 metres of gain on a broad ridge with occasional rock steps. No exposure of consequence but wind on the ridge is the variable. 60 minutes.

Summit. Cairn with a view north to Seilandsjøkelen and south across the fjord to the Alta mainland. 30 minutes on top, weather permitting.

Descent. Same route. 2.5 to 3 hours. Budget the ferry return with a 90-minute buffer.

The turn-back rule

Wind over 15 m/s on the plateau: turn back before the ridge. Cloud closing with visibility under 100 metres: turn back immediately. Late on the ferry schedule: turn back and re-plan. Missing the last ferry on Seiland in autumn means a cold wait.

Gear

  • Proper hiking boots.
  • Full waterproof shell, windproof, insulating layer.
  • Map and compass. Turkart 1:50,000. Phone GPS as backup only.
  • Head torch.
  • 2 litres water, food for 8 hours.
  • Bivvy bag if the forecast is marginal.
  • Ferry schedule printed or screenshotted.

Fjellvettreglene applied

All nine rules. Load-bearing: Rule 1 (plan, and tell someone on the mainland your return ferry), Rule 3 (check weather and marine forecasts — ferries cancel), Rule 4 (self-rescue equipment in remote country), Rule 7 (map and compass, not phone), Rule 8 (turn back in time).

Logistics

ItemDetail
FerryHakkstabben or Bekkarfjord to Seiland. Check Boreal / Torghatten Nord timetable.
Drive from Alta90 min to Hakkstabben
SeasonJuly to mid-September, dry weather window
Phone signalPatchy on the upper plateau. Do not rely on it.
Emergency112. Evacuation from Seiland means helicopter — hours, not minutes.

Who should do Storvikaksla

Experienced hikers comfortable with a map and compass, with recent Norwegian mountain time, and with a full weather-window discipline. Anyone who wants a Finnmark day without guided glacier travel.

Who should skip it

First-time Finnmark visitors. Anyone who has not done a DNT Red before. Anyone without a full ferry plan and a backup plan for the ferry cancelling.
storvikaksla-seiland-ridge-hike.featuredImageAlt: Empty Finnmark plateau terrain with snow patches and distant glaciated peaks
storvikaksla-seiland-ridge-hike.metaDescription: Storvikaksla hike guide, Seiland island: 750m DNT Red ridge day west of Alta. 14 km, 750m gain, 6 to 7 hours. Ferry logistics, navigation, turn-back rule.

knivskjelodden-nordkapp-hike.title: Knivskjelodden: the actual northernmost point of mainland Europe
knivskjelodden-nordkapp-hike.subtitle: The tundra walk to the real northernmost promontory, 1,450 metres north of the Nordkapp tourist plateau. 18 km round trip, DNT Blue, sign-in book at the point.
knivskjelodden-nordkapp-hike.excerpt: Knivskjelodden is the actual northernmost point of mainland Europe, 1,450 metres further north than the famous Nordkapp cliff. 18 km round trip, 4 to 5 hours, DNT Blue. Tundra walking, no entry fee, sign-in book at the promontory. June to September only.
knivskjelodden-nordkapp-hike.content:
Nordkapp gets the photograph. Knivskjelodden gets the truth. The famous cliff with the globe monument is not the northernmost point of mainland Europe — Knivskjelodden is, 1,450 metres further north and accessible only on foot. 18 kilometres round trip across open tundra. No ticket office. No souvenir hall. A sign-in book on the promontory and nothing else.

This is the hike for travellers who want the actual northernmost point rather than the marketed one. It also happens to be free.

The honest grade

DNT Blue. The gain is modest (about 150 metres cumulative) but the terrain is genuinely exposed Arctic tundra. Wet underfoot in early summer. No shelter anywhere on the route. A weather window of 5 to 6 hours with no heavy rain forecast is the condition you want.

The distance is the factor people underestimate. 18 kilometres on spongy ground is slower than 18 kilometres on trail. Budget 4 to 5 hours, not 3.

The route

Trailhead. Signed car park on the E69, 7 kilometres before the Nordkapp tourist complex. Free parking, roughly 30 spaces.

Outbound. Marked T-path across open tundra. Gentle rolling ground with a few boggy sections bridged by stones. 9 km to the promontory, 2 to 2.5 hours.

The promontory. A metal sign-in book in a metal tube marks the actual northernmost point. Sign your name. The view is the open Barents Sea on three sides. The Nordkapp cliff is visible back to the south-east — you can see the tourist buses arriving at the plateau you are not on.

Return. Same route. 2 to 2.5 hours.

The turn-back rule

Heavy rain during the outbound: turn back. The ground gets worse, not better, under sustained rain. Fog: turn back if visibility drops under 100 metres. The tundra has no landmarks and the path fades in fog. Wind over 18 m/s: the return trip will be brutal and the promontory offers nothing to see.

Gear

  • Waterproof boots. This is the non-negotiable item. Trainers ruin the day.
  • Waterproof shell, windproof layer, insulating mid-layer.
  • Hat and gloves even in July.
  • 2 litres water, food for 5 hours.
  • Map and compass. Do not rely on phone GPS — reception is patchy.
  • Head torch outside peak midsummer.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 1: plan and tell someone. Rule 3: the weather off the Barents Sea is the variable. Rule 4: self-rescue equipment — Arctic tundra offers zero shelter. Rule 8: turn back on rain or fog.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadSigned E69 car park, 7 km south of Nordkapp plateau
Parking~30 spaces, free
SeasonJune to September
Drive from Honningsvåg30 minutes
Emergency112. Phone signal intermittent on the tundra.

Who should do Knivskjelodden

Travellers who want the real northernmost point rather than the marketed one. Anyone irritated by the 350 NOK Nordkapp plateau entry. Fit hikers who like empty terrain.

Who should skip it

Anyone short on time who only has the Nordkapp plateau in their plan. Anyone without waterproof boots. Anyone who cannot commit to turning back in rain.
knivskjelodden-nordkapp-hike.featuredImageAlt: The Knivskjelodden promontory at midnight sun, the true northernmost point of mainland Europe
knivskjelodden-nordkapp-hike.metaDescription: Knivskjelodden hike guide, Nordkapp: the actual northernmost point of mainland Europe. 18 km, 150m gain, 4 to 5 hours, DNT Blue. Free alternative to the Nordkapp plateau.

storfjellet-honningsvag-hike.title: Storfjellet: the Honningsvåg harbour peak
storfjellet-honningsvag-hike.subtitle: A DNT Green marked trail from Honningsvåg to 275 metres. 6 km, 2 to 3 hours. Views over the harbour and out toward Nordkapp.
storfjellet-honningsvag-hike.excerpt: Storfjellet is the harbour peak directly above Honningsvåg. 275 metres, 6 km round trip, 2 to 3 hours, DNT Green. Marked trail from town. View across the harbour and out toward the Nordkapp plateau. Snow-free mid-June to September.
storfjellet-honningsvag-hike.content:
Storfjellet is the local hill above Honningsvåg. 275 metres, marked from the town itself, DNT Green. This is the hike for a Hurtigruten turnaround afternoon, or for the day you arrived on Magerøya and want to stretch your legs before the Nordkapp run tomorrow morning. Short, honest, and it gives you the harbour from above — which is a better photograph than the plateau globe.

The honest grade

DNT Green. Marked T-path, steady climb, no scrambling. Weather on the plateau is the variable — fog rolls in off the sea fast, and a 275-metre summit on Magerøya can be in cloud when the harbour is clear.

The route

Trailhead. Walk from central Honningsvåg to the signed path above the school. No parking needed — the trail starts within 10 minutes of most hotels in town.

Ascent. Marked T-path through scrub and open slope. 275 metres of gain in 45 to 60 minutes.

Summit. Open cairn with views over Honningsvåg harbour, the Hurtigruten dock, and out toward the Nordkapp plateau on a clear day. The town looks small from above.

Descent. Same route. 40 minutes.

Gear

  • Hiking shoes or trainers in good weather; boots in shoulder season.
  • Windproof layer.
  • Water.
  • Phone for photographs — the harbour view is the point.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 2: adapt the trip to conditions. Storfjellet is the correct answer when weather has cancelled the Nordkapp plateau visit. Rule 3: check yr.no before starting — the summit can be in fog when the town is clear.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadCentral Honningsvåg, above the school
ParkingTown street parking
SeasonMid-June to September
Emergency112. Honningsvåg medical centre in town.

Who should do Storfjellet

Cruise passengers on a Honningsvåg turnaround. Travellers with a half-day spare on Magerøya. Families, mixed-ability groups.

Who should skip it

Travellers who want the big day — do Knivskjelodden instead.
storfjellet-honningsvag-hike.featuredImageAlt: Viewpoint above Honningsvåg harbour looking out toward the Nordkapp peninsula
storfjellet-honningsvag-hike.metaDescription: Storfjellet hike guide, Honningsvåg: 275m harbour peak. 6 km, 275m gain, 2 to 3 hours, DNT Green. Short afternoon hike straight from Honningsvåg town centre.

tufjordfjellet-magerya-coastal-hike.title: Tufjordfjellet: the Gjesvær seabird coast hike, Magerøya
tufjordfjellet-magerya-coastal-hike.subtitle: A short coastal DNT Green hike above Gjesvær on the west side of Magerøya. 5 km, 280 metres, 2 hours. Seabird colonies on the Gjesværstappan reserve below.
tufjordfjellet-magerya-coastal-hike.excerpt: Tufjordfjellet is a short coastal hike on the west side of Magerøya above Gjesvær. 5 km round trip, 280 metres, 2 hours, DNT Green. The Gjesværstappan bird reserve sits directly offshore — puffins, gannets, kittiwakes. Best combined with a morning bird safari.
tufjordfjellet-magerya-coastal-hike.content:
Gjesvær sits on the wild west side of Magerøya. Off the coast, the Gjesværstappan islands form one of the most important seabird reserves in the Barents region: puffins, gannets, guillemots, kittiwakes, and white-tailed eagles that hunt them. Tufjordfjellet is the short coastal hike from the village that gives you the colony from above, without a boat.

The honest grade

DNT Green. Marked trail, modest gain, no scrambling. Exposed to wind from the open sea — this is the westernmost ridge on Magerøya and the wind matters. Fog is common in early morning.

The route

Trailhead. Gjesvær village, 34 km from Honningsvåg by road. Park at the signed trailhead above the harbour. Limited spaces.

Ascent. Marked path climbing open slope with sea views growing the higher you go. 280 metres of gain in 50 to 60 minutes.

Ridge. Walk the ridge north for 15 minutes for the best angle on the Gjesværstappan islands.

Descent. Same route. 40 minutes.

Combine with a bird safari

Birdsafari Gjesvær runs morning boat trips to the reserve from the village harbour (roughly 650 NOK, 2 hours, May to August). Doing the boat in the morning and the ridge hike in the afternoon gives you the colony from sea level and from 280 metres above. Book the boat the day before — they run weather-dependent.

Gear

  • Hiking boots.
  • Windproof, waterproof.
  • Binoculars. Bring them. Puffins from the ridge are distant without glass.
  • Water.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 3: read the weather. The west coast of Magerøya catches weather first. Rule 4: equipment — wind on the ridge in fog is the exposure that surprises people.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadGjesvær harbour
Drive from Honningsvåg45 minutes
SeasonJune to early September (bird season)
Bird safari operatorBirdsafari Gjesvær, boat from the harbour
Emergency112. Nearest medical: Honningsvåg.

Who should do Tufjordfjellet

Anyone interested in seabirds. Travellers combining the hike with a morning safari boat. Photographers with a long lens.

Who should skip it

Travellers without a car — Gjesvær is not on the Nordkapp bus route.
tufjordfjellet-magerya-coastal-hike.featuredImageAlt: Gjesværstappan bird colony islands off the west coast of Magerøya
tufjordfjellet-magerya-coastal-hike.metaDescription: Tufjordfjellet hike guide, Magerøya: 280m coastal peak above Gjesvær. 5 km, 280m gain, 2 hours, DNT Green. Seabird colony views, pair with a morning bird safari.

duksfjordfjellet-magerya-inland-hike.title: Duksfjordfjellet: the quieter inland summit on southern Magerøya
duksfjordfjellet-magerya-inland-hike.subtitle: A DNT Blue tundra day above Duksfjord. 420 metres, 8 km, 3 to 4 hours. Empty terrain, reindeer country in summer, sparse cairns.
duksfjordfjellet-magerya-inland-hike.excerpt: Duksfjordfjellet is the quieter inland summit on southern Magerøya. 420 metres, 8 km round trip, 3 to 4 hours, DNT Blue. Exposed tundra, reindeer territory in summer, sparse cairns. You need navigation skills — this is not a marked tourist path.
duksfjordfjellet-magerya-inland-hike.content:
Most travellers on Magerøya see two things: the Nordkapp plateau and the E69 between Honningsvåg and the cliff. Duksfjordfjellet is a deliberate step away from both. 420 metres above Duksfjord on the southern end of the island, DNT Blue, with sparse cairns and open tundra in every direction. Reindeer graze the upper slopes in summer. You will not see another hiker most days.

This is the right hike if you came to Nordkapp and want one day off the tourist loop.

The honest grade

DNT Blue. The difficulty is not technical. It is the combination of sparse marking, open tundra with no clear line, and weather that can close in within 30 minutes. Anyone comfortable with a map and compass will find the route straightforward. Anyone relying on phone GPS in a 3G-patchy area will not.

The route

Trailhead. Park near Duksfjord at the small lay-by signed for the hill path. Limited spaces, roughly 6 to 8 cars.

Lower section. Faint path across open heath climbing toward a broad ridge. Follow cairns — they are spaced 100 to 200 metres apart. 250 metres of gain in 75 minutes.

Upper section. Open plateau with rolling ground and scattered tarns. Navigate to the high cairn. 45 minutes.

Summit. Small cairn with a view south across the fjord and north toward the interior of Magerøya. Reindeer often visible in summer — give them space.

Descent. Same route. 90 minutes.

The turn-back rule

Fog with visibility under 100 metres: turn back immediately. The cairns disappear and compass bearings become the only tool. If you are not practiced at a bearing-plus-pace-count technique, this hike is not for you in fog.

Gear

  • Waterproof boots.
  • Full shell, windproof, insulating mid.
  • Paper map. Turkart 1:50,000 for Magerøya.
  • Compass you know how to use.
  • Head torch in shoulder season.
  • 1.5 litres water, food for 4 hours.

Fjellvettreglene applied

Rule 1: tell someone your route and return time. Rule 4: equipment for self-rescue in empty terrain. Rule 7: map and compass, not phone only. This rule is load-bearing on Duksfjordfjellet. Rule 8: turn back in fog.

Logistics

ItemDetail
TrailheadDuksfjord lay-by, southern Magerøya
Drive from Honningsvåg35 minutes
SeasonLate June to mid-September
Phone signalPatchy. Do not rely on GPS apps.
Emergency112. Honningsvåg medical centre.

Who should do Duksfjordfjellet

Experienced hikers comfortable with sparse marking. Travellers who want a genuinely empty day on Magerøya. Anyone prepared to turn back in fog.

Who should skip it

First-time Arctic hikers. Anyone without map-and-compass competence. Anyone short on weather margin.
duksfjordfjellet-magerya-inland-hike.featuredImageAlt: Open Magerøya tundra with low peaks and reindeer country under Arctic light
duksfjordfjellet-magerya-inland-hike.metaDescription: Duksfjordfjellet hike guide, Magerøya: 420m DNT Blue inland summit. 8 km, 420m gain, 3 to 4 hours. Navigation required, reindeer country, sparse cairns.
